review_text: Was falsely accused by Kauffman last semester. Completely wrecked my relationship with UMD CS imo. He published his own statistics on AI cheating in his class which showed that at least 19.6% of the class was penalized with a grade reduction or a pending XF case (XF is an academic integrity mark which removes CS students from the major.) Even more students than that were accused. On one project, he mass accused and penalized 15% of the class for cheating using compiler-generated code or AI because they used an "advanced" command, even though it was listed in the class slides, project rubric, project description, textbook, and an approved linked webpage on his course site. He also said we could reference compiler-generated code for the project and even bring AI-generated notes and content to exams, which makes it really unclear about what’s actually allowed. He originally accused even more students for the same issue until realizing he had accidentally included a non-project-related file in his checker. He said he had high confidence in the accusation, and offered a 0 to those accused on the project (~2% overall grade reduction) or a one-on-one interview process where success would lead to a regrade request and failure results in a XF recommendation to OSC. Over 1/3 of the accused students still showed up despite the existential threat of taking the interview, which makes me believe that he was really throwing around accusations. Either way, Kauffman later wrote in a post that he "was never once convinced of a student's innocence during an interview" and those who were let go only had "plausible deniability." The process was extremely stressful and lacked transparency. At the end of his course, 20 students in his section went to OSC. For context, there were 50 AI-related OSC across all departments and classes at UMD for the entire year of 2023, meaning his section last semester would account for around half of that. Whether you believe it or not, I know many people who were accused, didn't cheat, and were penalized anyway, including myself. His passive aggressive comments about not giving extra credit on later projects because of the cheating cases and verbal/written rants about "cheating" students devaluing the CS degree before any of the cases were even resolved made the class feel toxic, distrustful, and stressful like everyone was constantly under suspicion. On top of the difficult content, the threat of being accused and kicked out of the major over one or two lines of code on a project that is flagged as suspicious could seriously impact anyone’s mental health. He may be allowing AI in future courses to avoid this issue again (or in his words, because too many people are "cheating"), but this is my two cents. 2 stars because I learned a lot in his class as mentioned by everyone else below me.

intro: insanely passionate professor that is always working on the class and how it works, pretty cool in general pros: - open note exams - fun lecturer that records lectures (i mainly had to watch but he is good) - good on answering questions, being available to students, even goes on piazza every now and then - gradescope homeworks that are quizzes, can retake - labs are useful to content - projects are fair, mostly public tests then manual inspection - extra credit available based on class engagement and some other small factors which helps with grades - constantly looks for feedback through surveys - piazza is good, incentive to discuss and engage with each other, prof will be on there at times - besides recordings/slides/example code, other things are also posted and are available for use - practice exam reviews a bit of the content, it is reviewed in lecture before exam (good overall) - makeup credit available on projects in a way - TAs are pretty helpful neutral - flow of class/content/lectures expects you to be on top of work constantly rather than doing it last minute including him asking questions in lecture to class - "required" textbook, available online when I took class, reading it is not actually necessary but it seems like very good supplemental material - discussion section not necessary to attend (when I took class) but offers good reinforcement on labs and content cons - one part of labs is a quiz section but you don't know what questions are right or wrong when you check the answers, so sometimes its hard to know what you did wrong and end up wasting extra time conclusion: take the class! he's a good professor

Amazing professor! All lectures are recorded, exams are graded fairly, labs encourage group work and actual learning. Rewards effort in the class through small extra credit/make up credit which can help your grade if you're 1 point away from the next letter. I would highly recommend him.

Great professor. While he can be a bit critical and passive aggressive, he does an outstanding job at actually teaching the course content. Each topic is explained in great detail, there is a ton of resources provided outside of lecture to study and review. The course itself is also quite fun, but can be challenging. Also open note midterms and final is pretty great. Highly recommend taking him whenever possible.

I'd like to start off by saying that he is not inherently the worst of the worst, I think that his approach at teaching is not the best, but I don't think he means it with ill-intent. I barely scraped by and ended with a 73% which resulted in a C, even after always scoring below the average on all his exams and quizzes. The way that the course was structured, at least when I took his class, was set up in a way that the projects and exercises made up a substantial part of the overall course grade. Lab works also made up a small but considerable percentage that can boost your grade by the time finals come around. If I am not mistaken at the beginning of the course, he acknowledged that his exams and quizzes were known to be difficult, but that as long as you completed the projects and exercises on time and tried your best in exams and quizzes you were going to be okay. Projects: His projects were not inherently difficult, they were at times confusing, but attending office hours and posting on Piazza ultimately cleared any confusions you may have had. Exercises could often be done in a day, but I would suggest not doing this for both exercises and projects. Lab works were also easy to complete, and we were given enough time to complete them. Just by completing these on time, you can be at a good standing before exams and quizzes tank you grade (and they will, unless you are the type to ruin the curve.) Exams/Quizzes: His exams and quizzes were confusing, tedious, and soul draining. Often times it was never truly clear what exactly we were going to be tested on. Before exams or quizzes, he would send out emails containing a list of what we had covered since the previous quiz/exam, and he would say that what was on the slides was testable material, so it would be hard to differentiate what exactly was going to be on the test/quiz. On one occasion, what he stated was going to be covered on a quiz were assembly function calls and parent-child processes; given the difficulty and notoriety of his quizzes I studied everything assembly and assembly-function call related, and only studied parent-child processes some half hour before the quiz and ultimately the quiz only contained one assembly multiple choice question and the rest of the quiz was all related to parent-child processes; this time the quiz was easier than expected, but the angst and confusion leading up to the quiz takes a toll on you. Part of the shortcomings related to quizzes and exams *could* be attributed to a student's lack of complete familiarity and understanding of the material, however, unless the only thing you have going for you is school (and that is okay, great actually, if it's the case for you - it is not often, rarely actually, for that to be the case for everyone) then it is very difficult to develop mastery of the material in time before exams and quizzes. Some topics may be covered a lecture or two before an exam/quiz and some of the questions on quizzes and exams feel like they are meant to be 'gotchas' or may be super specific to a covered topic. For example, though the understanding of unix commands is useful in various industries, some questions often relied on a lab discussion slide that may have shown some [blank] unix command that was only used then and never really again - to some students those questions may have felt like free points, but when you are handling other courses and only have a vague idea of what was to be on a quiz then, some miscellaneous unix command would feel like the least of your concerns. I personally scored poorly on exams and quizzes, at some point getting a 39% on an exam, fully tanking my grade and putting my chances to pass on the line. However, because I submitted all other projects, exercises and labs on time, my grade was brought up just enough to pass. Yoon as a person: Results may vary, but Yoon was extremely approachable and a kind understanding person. If you come to his office hours or email him, he is very helpful and he often responds to emails fast. I often stayed after class to ask clarifications or questions related to the content, so did others, and he would make sure to explain and breakdown your confusions so as to help you better understand the material. Moreover, if you maintain a clear communication with him about certain issues and difficulties, he is often willing to help and possibly grant small extensions for submissions. He is also kind of funny when you talk to him one on one. I would say, do not walk into his class seeing him as some sort of evil supervillain who sits in his office petting a cat while plotting on how to make his next exam as hard as possible. If I am not mistaken, being a professor is not his main role at the university, take a look at his extensive resume and his achievements and you sort of realize that being a professor is just a role he has to play, and he can only have so much time on his hands, so balancing all of these responsibilities may be difficult even for him. p.s. I say all this not to glaze him or hype him up, all I'm saying is that it's probably a lot of work to handle but he's kinda goated if you talk to him - ppl like to make him seem like the devil himself but he's not too bad. Yoon as a professor: Now, regarding his teaching style, it is not really the best. Attend all his lectures and you will realize that not many people show up. He likes to go into a lot of specific niche topics while teaching, and he likes to draw on his iPad to breakdown what may be happening regarding memory allocation and a lot of other visual stuff. Often times this gets confusing, as he draws on top of the slides, so it is not visually the best and easiest thing to understand unless you follow along closely. If you walk into class during these moments when he draws out topics, it looks like that one picture 'my coworker losing his damn mind.' Moreover, sometimes he goes back and forth with a lot of coding examples and slides, so it gets a little confusing to understand. It is hard to keep up sometimes and it would often require revisiting his recorded lectures to get a better grasp of a concept. On the times he does do well, then it is easy to follow along and he does a good job at walking you through some of the topics. Class logistics: Honestly not the best at all. This semester, we only found out about our exam results a couple of days before the final. Some projects take a long time to get graded, and the late submission turns on after 11 pm of the on-time submission date. Some lab works were graded the day of grade submissions, so it was difficult to truly get what your grade was throughout the semester. Honestly, it was gut wrenching not knowing where you stood before the final exam was added to the grade book. He would often curve, at some point in some semester he curved by 8% percent, but this semester he did not curve as much, only slightly so that was also gut wrenching. TLDR: Not the worst. His exams and quizzes are hard and may tank your grade, but the class is set up in a way where your grade is able to take it if you do all the other work on time. As a person he's chill, as a professor he struggles, your grade will never be truly clear until the day grades are due. If you have to take him because every other section is closed prepare for a rough semester but if you show up and do your best you should be good. Not truly the end of the world, but you will have to put your head down to make it through.

He teaches pretty well, you come out of the class with a good understanding of 216 content. The worst part about this class are the projects, they're long and hard, start early. Tons of people get caught using GPT for the projects, but he's talked about somehow implementing it for future semesters to be more AI friendly/accommodating. Final Grade average: 85.58 Final Grade median: 87.79

All of the projects, homeworks, labs are very useful and relevant for the exams. Nothing in this class is that difficult, but make sure to start on all projects as soon as possible. Kauffman is always heavy on catching cheating on the projects; just give them your best honest attempt and I promise you'll be fine. His slides are very wordy and often contain a lot of useless information, and you're meant to rely more on your own work for the exams (they are open resource). Kauffman is by far the best choice for 216 right now and it's not really close.

A phenomenal amazing passionate considerate professor. Kauffman provides you with all the resources you need to succeed. He clearly cares a lot. - incredibly fair exams (open note too) - detailed and well-structured projects - very quick Piazza responses - lecture and lab/project walkthrough videos on Panopto - leniency with some late projects - so many opportunities for bonus points / extra credit / ways to save your grade I’d recommend him 1000x

Amazing Professor! Lectures are very engaging and he explains things clearly. Grading is very fair and he gives lots of opportunities for bonus/make up credit. Labs and HWs are relatively easy and focus on introducing concepts. Projects are hard but not impossible and they truly teach you the material. Exams are fairly difficult but they do reflect what's taught in class and are similar to the practice exams given. If you study hard and understand the labs and projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. Re doing labs on paper or parts of projects is a great way to study, the exams usually have pretty normal coding questions with the rest being short answer. The short answer matters just as much as the coding in terms of grading which isn't bad by any means. He gives extra credit for participating in lectures and answering questions on piazza which is a great idea!! I initially was motivated to participate for the extra credit but after the first few weeks of class, I found myself participating out of genuine curiosity or just wanting to engage with the material. For piazza, this was the one class where students would always respond to questions quickly which was very helpful. Overall, Kauffman is a great professor and I'm glad I got to take this class with him!

There's a lot of things that Kauffman does well like the format of the exams (open note, open terminal) and the instructiveness of the projects (the project descriptions are very thorough). He also records lectures and really tries to make sure that you can do well in the class. However, the main reason I took off 3 stars is because of all the AI accusations he was throwing around. Like I was not accused, but I know so many people who were falsely accused of using AI and that literally made it so scary just to go to class. Even though I wasn't doing anything wrong, I always felt like I would be falsely accused. I think that this part of the course needs to change because while it is important to ensure that the class is fair for students who actually do their work, I think that before Kauffman goes around accusing people, he needs to be 100% sure they actually did something wrong.

Kauffman is an incredible professor and 216 is pretty easy with him. His lectures are incredibly detailed and informative. The structure of the class is very well made and makes it easy to understand everything. Every assignment given gives enough practice for the exams (you should not chatgpt the projects for this reason) so you don't need to practice much outside of the coursework and if you didn't understand something in lecture the labs and projects will usually fill these gaps. The projects only have public tests and the exams are open laptop so you can download anything you want for the exam just no google or ai. One warning I'll give is he told us he would reorganize the grading structure to deal with ai cheating and his options were either lower project weighting and raise exam weighting, add an extra midterm, or automatically fail anyone who's exam average is below 70%. Personally I don't think these should be an issue due to what I've written in this review. If you're reading this and picking classes definitely take this guy over anyone else

Genuinely horific class. There's a ton of reviews that elaborate on why he's so terrible but it really does not compare to how awful it is to actually be in this class. Please, PLEASE do not take Yoon if you can help it. Hell, even if you would have to delay taking this class, that might be preferable if you're already struggling mentally. I mean this seriously. Projects are poorly documented and extremely tedious. Classes are unbelievably boring and nigh unfollowable with how poorly Yoon can actually qualify what he is teaching. Quizzes and exams consist of either extremely tedious coding, very niche knowledge, and the memorization that'll have you wondering whether anything you learn in this class will actually stick with you: it won't. On multiple ocassions you will think this class has drained your passion for CS. I can know state with certain that I still like CS, but that Yoon has doon irreperable damage to my view of UMD and any form of education it could teach me in the subjects that actually matter to me. If this isn't enough to convince you, Kaufman apparently has open notes. Avoid Yoon at all cost.

Nicole is the only reason why I am still alive in 216 w/ Yoon. She is super nice and helpful and I wish she was our lecturer because her explanations always make 100x more sense than those given by Yoon. She is goated

I genuinely like Yoon as a professor and of the CS professors I've had at UMD so far, Yoon is my favorite. Though it's obvious I'm odd in that. He's really nice and will happily answer any questions you have, and its very clear he cares about teaching the class and students success. I don't think there's anything that feels unfair at any point, and he often puts up clarifications (or honestly borderline hints) for exams/quizzes part way through for common clarifications. I think the projects could have clearer explanations on the assignment docs, but I think some of that comes down to real-world edge cases or behavior vs what they want to and can test in a class project sometimes not lining up exactly. I will say, the TAs were very hit or miss this semester and any real complaints I do have about the course come down to that, since the grading can take a while and some of the responses on piazza were not the best.

If you haven't already, go look at that rating and the amount of reviews. On god it is not a lie, he deserves it. When I tell you his class will be a detriment to your mental health and academic career I'm not joking. I'm going break down why though: 1. His teaching style is to essentially overload you with a bunch of information, most of which isn't relevant for the upcoming task such as project or test/quizzes. So while you're busy trying to sift through what you need and don't, along with trying to understand the topic at hand, he's already moved on to the next. Which is infuriating because I'm paying thousands of dollars for this to be my experience. 2. Remember what I said about this unnecessary things he'll teach in class? Yeah he'll make those small things important questions on his test/quizzes. 3. He will, and I mean will, overload you with work. The best way to put it, he operates as if his class is the only one you're taking. To give you an example, literally the night of our first exam, he assigned the next coding project and told us "I suggest you get started on this immediately". I got the notification for the assignment literally late night while me and a friend were studying for HIS exam that was going to happen later that day in Mckeldin. As you can image we were pissed. And he also gives little-to-no time between projects to breath. 4. Speaking of the projects, his project documents are longwinded and vague. So much so that the piazza is littered with people asking for clarification on things that should be crystal clear constantly. And you can get a bunch of points docked if you miss just 1 of the several details he gives you in his, on average, 7 page docs. Which if you ask me, it makes no sense that you have a doc that long and people in droves are still asking for basic clarification. You wrote all that for nothing then. 5. The TAs are hit or miss, and it's almost dramatic. Some are really cool and helpful, while others feel as if they think just being there while offering little to no should guarantee them a check. I had to hassle one of them to tell me what was wrong with my code for like an hour as I obviously didn't know and tried almost everything at that point. And once they finally helped me, it took them like 10 minutes to find the issue and relay it to me. There has also been multiply times where TAs will change their hours without updating the google calendar, and as a commuter it's just rage inducing. And I've brought this to Yoon's attention before and he acts as if it's a trivial matter. Like bro I'm wasting gas and time and he's so tight with his deadlines it makes no sense that he's chill about this. (Now the TA thing isn't that important since they're subject to change, but considering he chooses who becomes his TA, I think it's still worth mentioning). 6. His late policy. I don't think it's bad but I think it's worth mentioning and from here you can decide whether you're cool with it. So essentially you can turn in a assignment late only 24 hours after it's initial due date. So if it wad due the 21st at 11pm (yes all his projects are due at 11 pm, not 11:59pm), then the late due date is the 22nd at 11pm. And he will reduce the overall grade for this assignment by 15%. But don't get this confused with the GFA requirements, where if you don't meet them for each project you will be dropped by a letter grade. You can go back for any project to meet the GFA requirements (which could be to just pass all public test for example), but you won't get a new grade for that assignment. So if you get a 75% on that project, that's what you're stuck with. In all, please just avoid his class. It's unnecessary stress and I'd recommend that you don't pair this class with cmsc250. Take one or the other, especially if you can't escape his class for whatever reason (like me). And sure he has a seemingly nice personality and all, but if he was truly as nice as people say I don't think he'd work his students to the bone the way he does. It's needless and truly from my experience, I won't be able to say with confidence that I know how to code C by the end of this class since I and many others just understand enough to get the projects out of our faces and to get barely passing grades on his quizzes and exams. So if you want to actually know what you're doing, don't take him. GOOD LUCK!

He's quirky but a great lecturer (not a professor). Our exam Averages were lower than Kaufman who was teaching 216 at the same time, which is something to keep in mind. But imo the exams were generally difficult, but fair and he gives you a lot of resources to prepare for them ahead of time.
Larry Herman kindled my love for systems programming. He models what a lecturer should be: engaging and clear. I see now after many years that he's one of UMD's best.
First exam took me from feeling like I'd get an A to feeling like "lets pray for a curve to a C-" even after doing great on the quizzes. Class is abuse.

I want to say: I've gotten 90-100% on everything so far, this isn't a grade thing. The only avg I can see for quizzes so far is quiz1, which is ~20/30 so right in line with what people say of "most people technically fail every assessment.", so take my own grade with a grain of short term salt. I hate writing reviews mid-semester for classes since I think it can come off as salty, but given he's teaching the class again in Spring I must do my part for students registering for classes lol. Yoon is actually a nice person. BUT. The class quality is genuinely atrocious, I wanted to disagree with the previous reviews but really the fact that this class is costing me money is rather depressing. I don't personally have any issue with the lectures but I also don't pay any attention during them, since skimming the slides is usually plenty, but that really doesn't excuse the teaching quality. The projects aren't that bad, as long as you sit down and do them with a couple days of time to work since they can involve a lot of writing code even if its straightforward. My only issue with the projects is the same issue I have with quizzes and assume I will have with the exams: the requirements (or on assessments the phrasing of questions) is usually so poor that they simply don't make sense or are missing key details for you to do the work. It's been the case on every project so far that there is an absolute deluge of posts asking "what does this actually mean" about project specs, because the phrasing of a spec will be vague enough that you could interpret it many different ways and the public tests will be so incredibly simple that they don't address a single one of the clarifications needed. There's also often not actually any details given on expected formatting for things that require formatting, and TAs/instructor will sometimes give conflicting answers with each other to questions. Whether I end this class with a C- or an A+ I'll leave having learned the exact same amount from the lectures and projects: absolutely nothing. The fact UMD is ranked as high as it is for CS and then has professors for lower level classes that cannot teach and TAs which vary from "make the class easy as pie" to "Lecture round 2 with no benefit to the students at all" is rather insane. You would think it in the school's interest to attempt to give students a strong foundation so they can confidently get internships, do research, do well in later classes, but I'm pretty sure the avg student coming out of this class will only have gained the knowledge that "they don't like C/assembly" based on a bad prof.

Oh Yoon, Oh Yoon, where do I start? This was single-handedly the most BEAUTIFUL class I've ever taken during my long, long, 7 years at College Park, Maryland. Yoon explains things so beautifully even a monkey could understand assembly and linux. I'm convinced the 85% of the class that gets below an A has an iq between 41 and 67. I showed my pet goldfish the Yoon lectures (he didn't record them, so I uploaded them myself using my neuralink) and he showed up to the lecture and got a 97 on the final. He invoked such an incredible passion for low-level programming in me I decided to skip the entirety of my undergrad and do a PHD at Tsinghua in compilers. I came to class every day, and he handed me extra points for simply existing, because he's such a beautiful soul. GOON WITH YOON! #YOONERS
Super nice professor but I'm not sure if I really learned anything because his exams were practice test simulators. Projects aren't too bad tho! He's also very entertaining to watch.

honestly this class was pretty hard in the sense that i swear not being able to use computers or phones in class made it impossible to follow along with the code he taught us. on top of that lectures were so hard to follow because it was so slow and monotone other than the slight jokes he made. his worksheets were completion which was helpful but they were random so you had to attend every lecture and discussion or else youd get a zero not to mention you literally can not email him at all or he gets so upset, everything gotta be at his office hours or for absenses you have to fill out a form so its kind of difficult to communicate but he was funny and to be fair his tests werent too hard to pass, and his project guidelines were extremely thourough (for them being 10 pages long minimum id expect them to be) as long as you go to every single lecture since he doesnt post his stuff other than very vague slideshows anywhere

taking the summer session currently, and it's making me want to kill myself! lectures are ramble-y, examples are far too busy, and the way we're being taught assembly (ie reading off slides verbatim) is absurd. exams and earlier projects were light but holy shit is this class miserable currently

Really well ran course. The projects were pretty instructive, especially the Assembly project. Considering that the tests are borderline open internet, they're surprisingly easy, and if you can do the projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. There's so many places to get bonus points, so even if you lose some points here or there, you can get them back really easily. The lectures can be a bit slow at times, but physical attendance isn't mandatory so if that's an issue you can just watch the lecture videos at your own pace. He loves answering questions, so if you're struggling with a concept, he'll make sure you understand it. Taking 216 with Kauffman really is a no-brainer, just do it.

If you want an easy grade without really learning anything, take Nelson. He tries to be funny, but that's not what I came to school for. I wish I had taken 216 with somebody who actually cares more about teaching than just being popular. I got an A, but I don't really feel prepared for upper level courses that might depend on 216.

pros: smart dude, good demonstrations, worksheets,projects,participation is grade padding, and final exam questions are very similar to the midterm and practice exams. cons: no phones no laptops during lecture, he wastes probably 5 minutes each class stuttering or calling people out for using their phone. unforgiving n strict, slow at grading, he gatekeeps online communication and office hours behind a fat block of text and rules u gotta read (this one not that bad just annoying). multiple choice section on exams is notoriously difficult and specific, he also DOCKS POINTS FOR WRONG ANSWERS which is apparently to "discourage guessing" but inherently makes every second guess themself lol. we had a failing midterm average. lectures are also incredibly boring but thats expected for any cs class ig. he did curve final grades idk what the dude 2 below is sayin looking back i would take an 8am to avoid larry in a heartbeat but thats jus me

One of the best professors I have ever had. I think he truly structures his course through a student's perspective. The fact that he records his lectures and lab walkthroughs really allowed me to fully understand the course concepts. I feel like he gave adequate practice materials for exams and although they were REALLY long, his projects were one of the biggest reasons I did well in this course. His long and intensive projects covered basically everything that we would need to know for the course and doing the projects helped me not only understand the topics conceptually but also in practice. My advice to future students would be to really understand the projects because thats usually what the coding question for exams will be based off of.

One of the best professors I've had yet. I thought his lectures were well-paced, informative and easy to understand. He is an engaging and personable lecturer who takes time to answer questions and his participation system provides a nice padding to your overall grade if you happen to need that. The later projects were ROUGH but not impossibly difficult with a reasonable amount of effort, starting on them early is definitely for the best. His open note exams (which I greatly preferred) test your ability to apply concepts instead of memorize random details, for obvious reasons. I def think your grade reflects the amount of effort you put in, and honestly this class was much less stressful than 132 for me.

Great professor who cares about his students' learning. Great class structure with lots of flexibility, difficult but rewarding projects, and easy but not free exams. You get what you put into this class.

This class wasn't too bad, but it really did not feel as rewarding as how the other reviews said. The structure of the class was split upon 5 projects, 2 exams and a final, hw "quizzes" (they're incredibly easy), and labs. The projects were quite difficult in my opinion (3 and 4 were the worst but in different ways I'll explain later), but they were good practice. PLEASE DO NOT GPT THEM, AND PLEASE START THEM EARLY (trust me you will thank me). They are designed in a way that is quite different from 132, they are very long and test the information covered very thoroughly, but it is good preparation for the exams. I thought projects 1, 2, and 5 were relatively straight forward with a decent difficulty, project 3 was pretty difficult because Assembly is just like that, but project 4 was really hard because it was assigned late (giving us less time to do it), but it was an added difficulty to figure out which tests were actually passing because it would work half of the time due to the concurrency (sometimes, tests would pass on grace, but completely fail on gradescope). The exams were actually not that bad, they tested content covered in the most recent project to the date of the exam, and were open note. But I think the open note concept is kind of misleading, it doesn't make the exam easier, it just made it a different kind of test, covering applications of topics rather than fundamentals which you may be used to from previous courses. The labs and hw were good practice if you diligently did them, and were overall pretty free grades. Kauffman did pretty unique system for participation called engagement points, which was counted under the labs you completed, but any extra lecture participation or piazza participation would be counted as bonus, which was pretty nice. The content covered wasn't really too difficult in my opinion, but I felt lectures were pretty pointless. His slides I felt made the content way more complicated than it needed to be, and his speaking was way too technical and not easily digestible. During discussion, a lot of times it felt the TAs couldn't really give me in depth answers for questions, and mainly just went over the labs. As a result, most of the time I would learn either reading the textbook or yt vids. A lot of times too, the way Kauffman focused on materials was more related to computer hardware than the coding, which made it feel more applicable for Computer Engineering majors, and I felt like the mateiral I was learning was kinda useless compared to how other teachers taught it. Some of the reviews talked about him being condescending, others talked about him being super nice, I thought he was fine overall in approachability, but like none of the extremes. TL;DR: Kauffman is a fine professor, but I felt a lot of the reviews are rating him higher than he should be. The course content isn't too difficult, but sometimes it was pretty frustrating using the terminal and grace, and its just overall a very different experience from previous courses. Once again DO NOT GPT PROJECTS, START THEM ON DAY 1 - you will suffer the consequences otherwise trust me.

Even though I struggled tremendously in this course, Kauffman seems to care about his students' success. He is especially engaging in lectures and extremely helpful during office hours and the staff are always there to help on OH, Piazza, and Discussion. I believe that if you put in the effort and dedication in this course, you will be able to do well. I will say, though, that you should start ALL of Kauffman's projects on day 1 because these projects are not easy, and they require a lot of focus and attention, and you will have to know the content very well. In addition, his exams are not easy, so make sure you really understand your notes, slides, projects, lecture code, etc. I will give him 3 stars, however, because he does make this class somewhat stressful, but I do think that these stressful experiences will continue in later CMSC classes (not to say that they're bad, but they will become more rigorous, and students will need to work harder to do well). Kauffman provides tremendous grade padding from manual inspection on projects and a fair engagement point system, which can tremendously help students improve their grades. If you are someone who is interested in low-level systems, then I believe that you should definitely take 216 with Kauffman, and you will like it !

He gives a lot of examples, but the multiple choice on his exams kill me lol. I think his one star ratings are wayyy too harsh. I didn’t study as much as I should have and that’s why I got a bad grade. GO TO DISCUSSION. Omg please the worksheets are helpful! Also, he goes into DETAIL for his project requirements. Read everything. My biggest issue with him was that I find his voice very easy to sleep to in lecture lol.
brother didnt curve this terrible class lmao last straw

as someone with 9 years of programming experience here are the pros and cons of larry the lecturer not professor: tldr: will you fail? only if you dont try your best pros: - practice exams cover most relevant material (enough to get an A) - he will actually give you all the material/instructions - he will repeat material that will be on a test multiple times in a row during lecture - all of the material is important (some people were falling asleep, this is the basics of computers, get used to it) - wont argue about ADS accommodations (just visit his office hours) cons: - annoying policies (not talking about attending class or electronics, that is normal. youre paying for the lecture hall, go to it??) - teaches some content only from you reading the textbook (only in 216) - slow at grading, not the TAs fault, hes just slow - wont let you email him (at least hes nice in office hours) - rambles a bit

Goes on rants during class and wastes valuable class time correcting students on their phones and trying to pick up from where he left off. He wastes like an average of 2 minutes per lecture doing this. Projects are horrendously long, he likes to throw stupid curveball questions on exams, won't teach certain chapters from the book and just assigns it as reading and then tests hard on it on exams like you're a jackass with a blindfold. Discussions are pointless besides picking up the worksheet, which most of the time can be done alone outside of discussion time. If you have a tight, hard schedule in your semester coming up, don't take this guy as he's like a greedy goblin taking up your valuable study time like it's gold.

Actually the worst professor ever. Teaches useless shit during lecture and repeats the same useless information over and over again. Forced us to learn assembly and other important concepts on our own. Exams are nothing like the practice. Averages on both midterms were 65 but blames the class instead when it says more about him. It’s been a week since the final and I still don’t know if I’ll pass the class or if he will curve like he said he would. What professor forces students to learn the main topics of the class on their own?

One of the worst experiences of my life. It's been a week since the final exam and I AM STILL WAITING to know if I pass this class or not. Whatever you do, AVOID HERMAN at all costs. Don't listen to the 5 star reviews gassing im up as they don't know what they're talking about. Herman made my second semester at UMD a living hell. Please do yourself a favor and avoid this prof.

Kauffman is so goated and beloved by all his students. He made content that I otherwise would've found relatively uninteresting (operating systems, unix processes, assembly) engaging. He is incredibly passionate about teaching and always takes time to answer questions during lecture, and also post lecture recordings as well as lab overviews. I learned so much from him this semester and you can tell he actually cares about making sure his students understand content. The projects can be difficult, and I'd recommend starting them early, as well as using Piazza as much as you can because office hours are pretty limited. Exams are also tough but his homeworks and labs give you good practice for them. Most people probably don't actually do the homework but if you want extra practice then you should do it. He also gives opportunities for extra credit as mentioned in other reviews. I think there is no better professor you can take for CMSC216 and with Kauffman you'll be in good hands.

I took Kauffman in Fall 2024, but I'm just writing my review now. He's a nice guy and is very knowledgeable about what he's teaching, but I'm gonna be honest people are glazing him way too much. First of all, he speaks very technically in class with heavy technical jargon and doesn’t break down new concepts in a digestible way, which makes it hard to follow. Additionally, his slides have so much content on them that it's hard to digest and understand what exactly you're supposed to look at. The good thing though, is that he gives lots of extra credit, which is pretty rare to see in a CS class, so that’s nice. Another thing is, no offense to him, but he seems a little socially awkward. I talked to him in office hours and after class, and he seems to have a hard time speaking at a normal speed and has trouble making eye contact (another thing I’ve noticed with a lot of CS professors at UMD—a lot of them seem autistic or socially awkward). Another thing is that he has a lot of trouble taking criticism. In his midterm survey, so many students pointed out that there is a big difference in the level of difficulty between his practice and real exams, but he claims that because he has way more experience and is smarter than us, he knows a lot more than we do and dismisses literally any criticism. Overall, you will get a good grade in this class if you put in the effort, but him as a professor is not the best choice.

It's clear that the following three reviews were all written by the same disgruntled student. Please take these with a grain of salt and do your own research before making any decisions. Personally, I had no issues with Kauffman and found him to be one of the best teachers in the CS department at this school. >> Honestly I haven't learned anything in his class for cmsc216 because he would never explain things properly due to his unintelligible English; his notes, slides, labs, and projects didn't help and he would purposefully put tricky questions on exams just to assume that people are cheating in his class. DO NOT take this class as it will ruin your emotional stability and cause immense paranoia like it did to me. Stay away from this man and protect yourself at ALL COSTS !!!!! >> very difficult and unhelpful lectures >> I don't think I understood much from Kauffman's lectures. His slides and lecture code was often hard to follow and he information overloads on his project docs, slides, and lab/hmw directions. Although some may find it helpful with given information, I often had a hard time understanding what exactly I am supposed to do with the given information and how I can learn/complete the tasks. I found his projects to be mentally breaking, and I don't agree that this is what is best for students. I believe this class took a huge toll on my mental health and emotional stability. but I don't think Kauffman's 216 would be a great learning experience as you would have to struggle for basically nothing. Last but not least, I don't even understand why we had to learn Assembly in his class because I find Assembly to be completely useless and no one uses it anymore in modern programming. Take this class with another professor if you can

Honestly I haven't learned anything in his class for cmsc216 because he would never explain things properly due to his unintelligible English; his notes, slides, labs, and projects didn't help and he would purposefully put tricky questions on exams just to assume that people are cheating in his class. DO NOT take this class as it will ruin your emotional stability and cause immense paranoia like it did to me. Stay away from this man and protect yourself at ALL COSTS !!!!!

If I could rate the guy higher than 5 I would. Prof K has probably the most amount of passion for teaching students I’ve witnessed. Expect a lot of work with weekly labs and homework’s in addition to 5 projects, but all of which help a ton with learning the content. Prof K rarely was unable to fully answer a question during lecture and he absolutely knows his stuff. Definitely recommend if you can take him.

Idk why his rating is so low tbh. Larry is super fair with the stuff he teaches if you go to lecture and actually pay attention you will be perfectly prepared for the exams. His projects are great because the descriptions get real specific about what you should do while also leaving some room for you to do it your way. 10 total points were free points of worksheets graded on completion that you just had to go to some lectures/discussions to complete. On top of this he curves. Getting an A in this class is easy if you regularly attend lecture and are half decent at programming.

Kauffman and I had our enemies to lovers arc this semester. At the beginning of the semester with the stuff introducing C, I felt he went too slow, I felt the labs were unhelpful, and I felt bored in the class. But once Kauffman got to Assembly, and especially once he got to the UNIX and hardware material at the end of the semester, he really opened my eyes to learning about computer systems in general. Professor Kauffman has an enthusiastic lecturing style---you can tell he cares a lot about our understanding of the material, and the labs and homeworks he provides really do give great practice for his exams. His exams are also challenging, but definitely fair, and the open resources were a nice touch that I genuinely used, albeit barely, but when I used it it was absolutely needed. There was never a question I did not know how to do on his exams given the practice material provides you with exact question types you might see. Professor Kauffman has a plethora of pop culture references as well which keep him feeling simultaneously like your old uncle and someone in the know. I would recommend anyone take Professor Kauffman if you value your learning and your education more than your grade. His projects are difficult, but manageable if you start early, and I never had problems submitting on time nor with the actual coding itself. If you put in the work, you will get the A you desire. I have to dock one star because he called my supreme overlord Larry Herman "Professor" Herman. Everyone knows the GOAT is an instructor, NOT a professor.

Seeing a lot of people downvote Larry just because he's one of the first CS professors that they have at UMD. Larry will literally baby and hold your hand throughout this whole course- he does this by giving discussion worksheets where problems are literally pulled for the exam, LONG practice material documents with questions the exam same format as the exam, and LONG LONG LONG project descriptions that highlight practically every single edge case that you ever need to consider. The worksheets are literally just free padding to your grade (just show up!), so I'm not sure why people complain about that. They're literally not even graded for correctness, it's just like do 3/4 of the worksheet and boom instant 10/10. Larry tells you every single thing that you need to know and even how to know it (like just reading chapter X in the specified textbook), and the people complaining here are just the ones too lazy to do it.

Do not take his class. Extremely boring lecturer who goes incredibly in depth on the topics and then expects you to pull out the most random piece of knowledge for his exams. His exams are also incredibly difficult, and he will punish you for not knowing the material by taking off additional points if you get some questions wrong. He then has the audacity to be surprised at why the class average is so low. You also can't skip class because there is a slight possibility of a worksheet in class, which is ultimately useless if you haven't been keeping up in the first place. The most I ever learned from this class was by doing the projects (they did teach me a lot about programming in C). Still, he also makes them incredibly over the top, and his directions are unclear, including unnecessary sentences, detracting from the main requirements or criteria you need to follow. That being said, his projects were generally easy to complete or learn to complete.

Herman is a great 132 teacher, but for the love of god, he CANNOT teach 216. 216 is already a hard course, but herman will make it harder. He will make it a living hell. Unannounced worksheets in every other lecture/discussion that if you miss enough can bring your grade down more than a project. Exams are nothing like practice material. Save yourself the trouble and avoid herman at all cost

I would absolutely take Kauffman again--you can absolutely tell he loves teaching and his students, and he comes off as very approachable (which is really nice when you have a question, over email or in person). The class / projects / tests are hard, but it's absolutely worth the time and effort. Has a really good engagement point system that gives you some grace on late project submissions if you, again, put in the effort. Very well organized system, posts all his slides, records all his lectures, etc. etc.

Decent lecturer. Can be condescending and say things are "easy" or "simple" about concepts students see for the first time which is not helpful to students learning. Released project 4 over a week late with a short deadline and project had so much testing bugs and issues that Piazza had hundreds of posts. Not sure why there are such high reviews.

no way people are giving this dude above 3 stars- the most boring lectures the weirdest exams with a mean and median of 60 please don't take him save yourself he doesn't teach jack expects you to read entire chapters before coming to lecture its a self learn class with ass exams
Amazing professor. Teaches well and is very passionate. Quizzes were easy, and the midterms + final were all fair.

THE GOAT! Very good lecturer, the quizzes are very similar to the homework and worksheets. Even his exams aren't very difficult if you do the problems from class and discussions. He does speak a bit fast, but it's a fun class, and he's super funny! Honestly, the reason why I got a bad grade was mostly on me.

Fawzi is a very good lecturer and breaks down topics very well. The quizzes and tests are honestly not very difficult, and there are never any surprises. Make sure you come to every class and pay attention during lecture, which is easy to do considering that Fawzi is an entertaining lecturer. Getting the grade you want shouldn't be too hard as long as you spend a bit of time reviewing for each test and go to discussion.

Very good lecturer. The negative reviews are just people coping. His expectations for the most part are reasonable for a proof writing class and the few times they're not it's not really a big deal; his rules aren't hard to follow. As long as you do the homework and pay attention in class to the examples you will do fine on the exams.

Pretty awesome guy, and I'm sure he's a great professor, but just not for 250. His lectures usually fall into one of two categories: a) Way too simple to learn anything meaningful b) Far out of scope of the class, and moreso on 351 topics and beyond. He emphasizes when he does this that these things won't be tested and are just good to know, but it takes away from actual 250 instruction heavily. This is another class where you'll probably get a useless discussion TA, but you still need to go for the weekly quizzes. Not a terrible class by any means, but with Teli, you might have to learn a lot of the content elsewhere (Justin's notes are still available online).

I don't understand why so many people dislike Fawzi. Personally, I think that he's an amazing lecturer who sets up all of his students for success. He explains everything very thoroughly, and has great lecture slides/examples. His lectures can get boring sometimes, but they're usually fine. His quizzes are very similar in difficulty, if not slightly easier, than the homework questions. Some of his exam questions are sometimes confusing, but they are also very similar in difficulty to homework questions and very doable. All of his exams and quizzes are usually graded within a few days, and the TAs provide pretty thorough feedback on Gradescope. The only reason that I ended with an A- instead of an A or A+ is because I stopped going to classes (got lazy lmao) after Exam #1. Though I was able to still do fine by studying the lecture slides, doing the homework, and asking ChatGPT for help, I recommend just going to lectures to keep yourself sane. Also, even though he said there were many students doing amazing in his class, my 88.3 still became an A-

I think Teli is alright, but still a good choice for 250. He doesn't explain things very clearly, but he makes up for this by going through a lot of examples in depth during lecture. You're left with a strong understanding on how to answer questions correctly, but with hazy knowledge on why you format the steps in the way you're supposed to, especially since some of the TAs will format their proofs or answer problems a bit differently than Teli did. It's not a problem for most of the semester--the midterms are incredibly easy, easier than the practice exams. The content in the course only really gets difficult between the second midterm and the final, and since the final isn't cumulative, there's no easy content from the beginning to pad it; but still very doable if you study well. The quizzes are sometimes easy, sometimes difficult--definitely study for those, especially as you get further into the semester. Homeworks are challenging, so don't slack off on those either. Overall, the negatives of his lecturing style don't really show themselves until you reach the end of the course, and even then just skimming the recommended textbook helps solidify the things he doesn't go over as much. I complain about his lecturing style a lot, but honestly he is a solid choice for 250 and I would recommend; put in the work, and it's not difficult to get an A.

Fall 2025 Pros: - Very funny and engaging guy. Seems like a cool guy to just hang out with - Explanations are very clear - Expectations for proof formats are very clear. He makes it very clear what you will need to do to get all the points on proofs - Homeworks are not graded, and keys are posted, giving you more flexibility - If you do all the homeworks and discussion worksheets, you will be fine. No outside practice needed. Cons: - Grading is potentially challenging: 80% exams, 20% quizzes - Weekly scheduled/announced (not pop) quizzes in discussion, but all of them are fair and reflect what is on the homework (either the most recent one, or the second to most recent one) Other: - Lectures are not recorded, as Fawzi expects everyone to come to class. But of all the lecturers I’ve had at UMD, Fawzi has the most valuable lectures, so I wouldn’t skip. Grade Statistics Exam 1: Median: 88% Exam 2: Median: 87% Exam 3: Median: 78% Final: Median: 76% Median course grade: 82% (~35% of people get A’s) Overall definitely take Fawzi. He teaches very well, makes it easy to do well, and is a chill/funny guy.

Pretty good professor! Lectures aren't super engaging but they get the job done. Concepts are explained pretty clearly and while I did leave some lectures confused, I found rewatching the recordings always helped me understand the material. All lectures were recorded and his handwritten notes showed up in the recording and they were also posted as pdfs to canvas. Homeworks were a bit difficult but were VERY helpful in preparing students for the exams. He gives weekly quizzes in discussions, always on Wednesday's, and these were alright. The pressure of a weekly quiz does force you to stay on top of the material which sounds bad but I felt it made it so that I barley had to study for the midterms since I had put effort into learning the material for the quizzes. The exams themselves are not overly difficult, they do reflect what's taught and have very fair grading. He also rounded up grades if you were 1% away from a different letter grade, and while that didn't benefit me, it was still very generous of him to do. In general, Teli is a very good professor and worth taking!

Great professor, great lecturer, and very enthusiastic about teaching the course content. You'll find that attending every lecture and discussion ensures you are gonna succeed in the class, and the weekly quizzes make it easy for you to stay on track with the content. Fawzi makes 250 easy by doing examples in class, and teaching you the general gist of the content and proofs in lecture. P.S. Fawzi can often be a pretty harsh grader, so go to TA office hours to understand where people often loose points in the exams and quizzes.

I took this course in spring 2025 and I can truly say that Fawzi was one of the best lecturers I've had at UMD. I learned so much from attending lecture and he explained topics very well. He would make explanations of topics very thorough and relatable so that they stuck. The discussions were not that useful in my opinion but that is very dependent upon your TA. However, the worksheets given in discussion proved to be very useful. I liked the fact that the homework was not graded because I could use the assignments for practice (I would do the problems then use the answer key to see what he was looking for in terms of format). Also if there was a problem that I could not figure out no matter how much I tried, I could always fall back on his answer key to point me in the right direction to start or learn how to do the problem entirely. Some might say that this "defeats the whole purpose of homework" but I would disagree saying that it made the homework less stressful and allowed me to learn something new rather than simply sitting there not knowing what to do. Overall his exams were very fair and were truly indicative of the content covered in class. Although he did not provide practice exams, reviewing the homework, worksheets, and lecture examples multiple times and ensuring that I was comfortable with those types of problems and the theory behind them was enough to do well. If you are on the fence about taking him, I would do it. If you attend his lectures, pay attention, and put in the work outside of class, you will do very well.

Lectures are fast paced and can be boring at times but attend them and PLEASE listen to him when he says "do the homework the day it's assigned". The homework is not graded so not mandatory but it's so helpful and if you ever have questions, you can go to OH and get help. Overall, wonderful person and a great lecturer. The exams weighting is a lot and small mistakes can lose you lots of points (how I ended with a B+) but I had friends end with A+ since they didn't make the silly mistakes I made. Just put effort and you'll pass easily. Overall wonderful lecturer for this class. 10/10 would recommend and take him again if I could.

teli is a pretty decent professor. 3 exams (2 midterms and 1 final). lecturing is alright, but overall lectures are pretty boring and hard to get through. quizzes are free grades and homeworks + exams are graded pretty strictly by TA's. shoutout to Shashaank who was genuinely the best TA ive ever had and probably will ever have. discussion is really what made this class for me and without it I probably would have done much worse with just Teli's lecturing. overall he's okay but you are probably better off with Fawzi.

Decent TA, could greatly improve with managing many students and ensuring that everyone gets equal time/attention. It seemed like there was favoritism to some students and/or that she was chatting with those students so she wouldn't have to help others like me. Normally I wouldn't mind this, I get being a TA can be tiring. It's just that I had missed a week of lectures due to falling badly ill, so I wanted to talk with a TA about how I could get back on track with the content. I told her all this and she told me I had to wait in line after which she chatted with two students at the same time for an half and hour before rushing through my questions in five minutes. Mind you, there were like four more students in line after me who were also waiting the whole time.

Decent professor. Definitely cares about what he's teaching an explains concepts really well. He is slightly slower and doesn't cover as much content as other professors though. Grade consists of weekly quizzes, homework, 2 midterms, and a final. Quizzes are pretty hit or miss, but eventually you get the hang of it. Personally, I felt the quizzes were harder than the exams cuz they kind of test you on trickier stuff. Homeworks are definitely hard but are good preparation for the exam in terms of what to expect. Start on them early and ask TAs about any questions you have to maximize your grade for them. Exams are pretty easy. If you just study for them, you're guaranteed a 90+. They are straightforward questions and even if you can't figure out a proof, you can reason your way to something to get most of the credit. Grading can be a bit harsh for homework assignments and sometimes the quizzes as well, but just submit a regrade or ask Teli and he can fix it. The final wasn't actually a final. It was just a third midterm. Only covered whatever we learned after the 2nd midterm. Was also about the same length as the other midterms, maybe slightly longer, but you get twice the amount of time. Overall, I thought it was a well ran class. Because it is a discrete math course, some of the things might not click immediately. But he records lectures so you can look back at them and if you go to his office hours, he is very open to answering any questions or doubts you have. I would definitely say Teli is a good option for 250

i'm decently lukewarm about teli. - his lecturing gave you the bare minimum in what you needed to know for hw and quizzes. his lectures can be a little wishy-washy within explanations - hw, discussion and quizzes were harder than the examples covered in lecture. had to spend a lot of time in OH figuring this out - exams were easy compared to the hw/discussions/quizzes. this is a redeaming quality of the class - TAs were a mess, regularly skipping OH, each TA would give you different answers to the same hw questions, partially like this because teli does not provide answer keys or grading guidelines to TAs - generally proof notes were sloppy, it makes it hard to figure out what is syntaxtically acceptable for an exam. grading felt random at times - recordings were a nice to have overall he is an okay professor, he get's the job done. 250 is generally taught a little bit confusingly, so if you're not a math person, plan to spend extra time in OH to figure this class out.
Great lecturer. Quizzes, homework, and discussions are all really helpful.

Alright professor. Teaches well and goes in depth on topics. Exams and really easy, much easier than the quizzes and homework assignments. Pretty similar to the practice exams he provides in terms of difficulty. The only downside are the quizzes. They are pretty hit or miss, and you have study a good amount to do well on it. They are a pretty small part of your grade though so it isn't too bad. Homeworks aren't bad but can take time to complete, so start early. The questions are pretty difficult and the format of answers can be a bit abstract. Definitely to office hours to make sure your doing things correctly. Overall, the class isn't too bad. Lectures can be a bit boring or confusing, but just watch the lecture videos and you should be fine. He's a really nice person and does a good job in explaining concepts. Would definitely recommend for 250l

Very boring lectures and sometimes doesn't explain the reasoning behind his work. You'll going to be doing a lot of self teaching compared to other classes because sometimes he'll mention an important topic or thing you'll need to know and never mention it again and sometimes skip it if its a step he taught. What makes it better is the lecture slides sometimes have no direct lesson or topic you pull out by looking at it. Instead you will need to go to his scanned pdf file of work done in class and scroll to find your answer you were hoping to get (Or sit there and open up the panapto recording and scroll through the hour and 15 minute video). Quizzes are relatively easy and thanks to the TA for babying our class or most would've struggled. The midterms are also really not like the exam or home works. They are a step above of what he'll teach or go over in class, some of it is already tricky but I really do not feel like spending hours everyday reteaching things that I need to get taught, not what I signed up. Takeaway: Knowledgeable dude but also so boring. Teaches like you already know all of the material. Do not take Teli for 250 unless you're into self teaching yourself a lot.

Max when I catch you!!! Do not take this class with him I'm begging you. He is the most unserious professor I have come across. And yeah he's funny... i guess! But really and truly just take another professor it will save you the headache. A hard class like this you need a serious professor and max is not it I fear...

CMSC250 is meant to be your introduction to proofs and mathematical rigor in Computer Science and I did not get that at all from this class. Lecture was relatively easy to follow and he was an engaging lecturer. However, he hand-waved a lot of things that I felt shouldn't have been handwaved. For example, we never really did structural induction like Fawzi's class did, instead he just kind of gave an outline of what it would look like. He is also a bit disorganized and him and his TAs were all five minutes late to one of our exams. There was a proof on our first exam that was unlike anything we had done up to that point which was rather annoying. Overall his class was fine, but I would recommend taking a different professor for 250 if Maksym is one of the options. His class may be easier to get a higher grade in, but it will come back to bite you when you have to write proofs for other classes in the future.

I don't get the hate with Fawzi. I followed his conventions and copied his style for all the proofs we did and I always received good grades. He can grade harshly, one of my friends definitely experienced that, I think it just depends on whether you fully understand the subject and how to do the proof properly. As long as you stay on top of things, you'll be fine, and he makes it pretty easy to do that. It helps to write your proofs the way he demonstrates in class. I do recommend going to every lecture, he shows useful examples, explains things well, and makes the classes interesting and funny. I recommend taking him for any course that you can.

i literally loved his class, but thats coming from someone who just loves riddles tbh, because i know our final hurt hella people who didnt know what was happening since everything was so vague also he does not curve or drop at ALL so expect your grade to be your grade imma be honest i took a makeup once and our TA didnt even show up til 1.5hours later which was kinda annoying but honestly its very passable

I do NOT recommend taking 250 with Teli. I got a decent grade, but I seriously don't know what I'm doing. I went to a couple of 250 lectures given another professor and it was so much better. The other class actually held my interest and now I understand what 250 is supposed to be like. Teli's version of 250 is more like a middle school class than a course at a university. I wish I had taken somebody else.

Fawzi is a solid lecturer, and he does a great job of preparing you for the higher level courses. Even though I'm not a strong math student, I feel like I now have a firm grasp of this material, especially writing proofs. Fawzi made it very clear what you needed to know for the exams, and the grading was pretty relaxed. Overall, this was a great class and I'd recommend Fawzi for any class you can take with him. As long as you put the effort in, you'll get a decent grade and you'll feel confident moving forward.

40% hw grade + 10% discussion grade so u essentially cannot fail, and pretty good lecturer when hes not realizing that his slides are completely wrong lmao. idk if this is good or bad but u can get by comfortably with almost never studying

He'll lure you in with a 40% homework grade (it's graded harshly), a 10% discussion grade(only free part of the course), and low weighted midterms and finals. The midterms were really bad, especially the first one. He never makes clear what's going on in the course. Nobody knew what the cutoffs for an A, A-, B+, ect were. Extra credit is almost worthless (0.05% per). He was extremely disorganized for all his lectures, he was late for 2 or maybe all 3 midterms so we lost valuable time (compensated with 0.5 extra credit points on a 32 point midterm). That being said. He's a chill guy and explained concepts in a really fun way. I liked his laid back approach focusing on more of the conceptual aspect as opposed to something more stingy on format (like Fawzi). If it weren't for the above issues he'd get 4 stars.

Fawzi was absolutely fantastic! He definitely expects students to come to the lectures, and it was so worth it! (Isn't that what we're paying for?) The lectures were fun and he made everything super easy to understand. Fawzi really cares about his students and does everything he can to make sure that they are really learning and not just going through the motions. He's preparing us for harder courses later (like 351). The solutions to the homework questions were always posted online, and we had plenty of materials to study (homework, worksheets from the discussion classes, and the lecture examples). He also gave out very high grades! Fawzi was amazing and I highly recommend him for any class he teaches!

The class is pretty easy as long as you pay attention in lecture. However, paying attention during lecture is difficult. Although his explanations were very good and he was a good lecturer, not having lecture videos made it really hard to fully grasp everything. I understand that he does not record lectures because he wants people to come to class but I feel like if he recorded his lecture, exam scores (especially exam 3) would have been much higher. He also does not post any practice exams which he says is because he does not want students to only memorize the exams instead of actually studying.

Did not like his teaching style. Old-school "come to lectures" method is annoying, especially with him not uploading answers to half of the lectures for the problems. Sign up for him only if you are ok with never seeing an answer key to practice problems.

My goat. Excellent and engaging lecturer and the homeworks tbh prepared me for the exam so well I never had to study anything else. The quizzes initially caught me off guard and I flunked the first few - I strongly encourage you to review the homeworks because he basically pulls the quiz problems directly from them. Course definitely intimidated me but he taught it in a way that made the information click really fast and stay relevant throughout the course. Honestly 250 with Fawzi was my favorite course yet.
Excellent lecturer, but not much else going for him. Abysmal grading system throughout the semester. If I took him again I would spend more time preparing for exams.

Fawzi might be the best professor I have ever had honestly. Coming into the class, the content seemed to be pretty intimidating, but he taught in a way that made everything stick completely. The grading was broken down simply by just quizzes and exams. I felt the exams were really easy, legit just testing if you understood the fundamentals of the topics he taught, with no real curveballs. Final was a bit harder, but that is to be expected. I will say though, lectures were pretty boring, because he would basically spend the entire time doing a bunch of practice problems, but I found this to actually be super helpful as I just learnt a lot more this way personally. Though, this made discussion virtually pointless, there would basically just be a worksheet, and thats about it. As for the quizzes, fawzi's policy would be that there may or may not be a quiz during discussion, as an effort to motivate people to go. But, this just basically turned into a lot of people coming just during the last 20 min of class, because quizzes would always occur during that time. As for Fawzi himself, some have said that he says some weird things during class (which he kinda does), but this didn't really affect me too much. Other than that, the way he taught just really stuck with me, and I felt like he really set us up well for the exams (averages were pretty high too). Definitely would recommend for this class.

I'm a weirdo who watches all my YouTube videos at 2x speed. Because of that, I found the pace Fawzi went at to be perfect for me. Doesn't record lectures. Otherwise, I found him to be a great professor who's passionate about the subject, but I recognize he might not be the best fit for everyone.

Fawzi is overall a very good professor. He makes his lectures fairly engaging and mostly I am able to follow. Don't be deterred by the "pop quizzes" in discussion. If you do the homework's and review his in class examples through out the semester (as opposed to cramming for exams), you will get 20/20 on most if not all of the quizzes. His exams are extremely fair. Sometimes on the homework there are quite difficult problems but these don't appear on quizzes/exams. Overall definitely take him if you can.

Great professor! I thought I would hate this class, but it was actually my fav last semester. His quizzes and exams are all pretty easy as long as you study what he covered in the lectures. He gave out pretty high grades on everything except Exam 3. All the other exams were graded very easily. Fawzi is pretty entertaining to listen to and I actually looked forward to this class. Can't say the same for 216. :-(
Bro's gotta work on his organization

This class was soo interesting. Loved it. He's a good professor. I definitely could've gotten an A, but his grading really messes u up. Each exam was out of 50 points so 1 simple probability question (10 points) wrong would literally bring you down to a 90%. My fault for getting a 10/20 on a quiz though. Like his exams are relatively easy BUT if u get something wrong you're lowkey done for. There are "pop quizzes" but honestly just expect a quiz every discussion or ask a friend. Take Fawzi! he's not so bad. He has a thing for telling us he's unprepared for class or doesn't wanna be there pretty often though lmao.

Maksym seems to be a very chill, albeit lazy, professor. The workload in his class is quite small, and he has a GOATED grading system with 40% homework, 10% discussions, and 50% exams. Do well on the hmws so that even if you mess up on the exams, your grade isn't going to sink like the Titanic
Nice guy, but BORING! I stopped coming to class because I just couldn't take it any more. I can't say I learned much. I don't recommend him for CMSC250.

Fawzi is the best professor in the C.S. department, hands down! I actually enjoyed going to this class. There are a lot of quizzes, but they were pretty easy. The tests were fair and not too long. Take any class with him that you can!

There’s no transparency about grading at all, he just put in final grades without even mentioning what the cutoffs would be, without adding in all the extra credit and without even telling us that they would be imported into testudo. This is just one example of the lack of transparency and the lateness of grading in this class.
disorganized but!!! he is humble enough to admit to when he is wrong and he works with you to succeed, and he's super chill and so kind

Fawzi is clearly really passionate about math and his enthusiasm makes the lectures pretty fun and engaging, imo. He's a good lecturer that communicates concepts pretty well, but he does go REALLY fast - fast to the point where you can't really take notes and absorb what he's saying at the same time. I think my biggest gripe about this class was the lack of resources and the way exams and quizzes were handled. Firstly, the lecture slides are super bare bones, to the point where you're kind of screwed if you miss class for whatever reason. Plus, he lectures so fast that it's hard to take detailed notes sometimes to fill in whatever is missing from the slides. This issue wasn't a big deal for some things like the proofs, where he gives us a lot of examples, but was a major problem in the probability unit. He also never provided the answers to the discussion worksheets, which were pretty important for studying for quizzes and exams (especially probability). Secondly, exams are super short, which might sound good at first, but a single mistake can cost a large chunk of your grade on the exam. Lastly, POP QUIZZES. These were by far the most annoying aspect of the class. You basically have to expect a quiz during every discussion. My discussion TA wasn't very good, so this made going to discussion even more painful.

Not a bad professor overall. Biggest con is that his lectures tend to be very disorganized. Other than that, his exams are fair and his grade breakdown is reasonable (40% homework, 10% discussion, 50% exams). His exams also contain more or less what he covers in lecture, so as long as you look at his notes or go to lecture you will be fine for the exams (the first exam did have a question that he didn't cover in class, but he later went back and curved it). Final was just a bunch of old problems that people didn't do well on in past midterms, which was a grade boost. One thing to note is that if you really do care about learning discrete math, I wouldn't take Max. His proofs are disorganized and lack structure, so if you'd like to learn how to write formal proofs I would stay away.

Max is a decent professor. His lectures were mainly discussing content and then doing lots of practice problems. Discrete math can be somewhat hard to teach as sometimes as a student you just have to get it. So honestly, he did a pretty good job at teaching content.
