My main complaint was the lack of consistency with grading and feedback from the TA's. The head TA Josh was very good, however. So ultimately, Max is a good professor. Just be prepared for some bumps in the road, but in the end, it'll all work out.

It's funny how the reviews for Fawzi are so divided! Let me make this easy for you: If you want to learn the CMSC250 material, then you 100% want to take Fawzi's class! He is the best lecturer (best teacher) I've ever seen. He's engaging, inspiring, and at times hilarious! If you're lazy or just not a good student and you want to pass CMSC250 without doing any work, without coming to class, and without learning anything, then he is not your guy. People are whining because Fawzi expects them to actually put in some effort! Maybe switch your major. Upper level classes are gonna be a lot worse. It's not really that hard to get an A in this class. You need to come to all the lectures, study the things he says are important, finish all the worksheets that are given out in the discussions, and keep up with the homework questions (which are not graded). The questions on quizzes and exams are totally reasonable and the grading is fair. The exam medians were always over 80%. He also gave quizzes that were super easy and totally predictable (if you've been keeping up). At the end of the semester he posted an announcement with the final grade distribution: 31% got an A+/A/A- 34% got a B+/B/B- 25% got a C+/C/C- 10% were D+/D/F What's the problem? This was my favorite class so far at UMD. I went in knowing nothing about how to write a proof and honestly, now I kind of feel like an expert at it!

Max is genuinely a very disorganized professor, his lectures at the beginning of the semester started off with lots of mistakes, errors, and he tends to go through material a lot quicker than necessary. Closer to the middle of semester he started getting better for lectures but it always felt like he came in unprepared for what he was going to teach and as if he didn't even review the slideshow he would be putting on the screen. Additionally there was a huge lack of timely completion for our homeworks and exams and it constantly felt like we were guessing what our grade in the class was. Piazza responses from the instructors were also so limited and it felt like the TAs also had no idea what was going on. Most of them barely responded to questions in Piazza. Grading: Pros: - very good weight distribution where 50% of the grade from discussion worksheets and homework - 3 exams and a final was nice because each exam was less weight (12%, 12%, 12%, 14%) - Extra credit (.05%) for things such as piazza posts, every 3 lecture notes and in class practices Cons: - exams were all over the place, with the first two exam averages being in the 70's range - first exam had a question that Max wasn't even able to fully solve in lecture - homework grading was much harsher than expected considering in lecture Max would often skip steps that the homeworks expected us to provide

Fawzi is my favorite professor I’ve ever had so far at UMD, at least as it stands through my first year. I’m going to preface this review: I took MATH310, another proofs class in the math department, a semester before this class, and as a result, knew almost all the proofs content (about 2/3 of the course) beforehand except for modular arithmetic. I still went to his lectures though. Full breakdown below: Lectures: Fawzi is probably the best lecturer I’ve had at Maryland. I had a subpar professor for MATH310, and as a result, struggled with proofs worse than I had struggled with any math class in my life (as someone who considered myself a math person). I managed to salvage a 78% in the class, but I was for sure no expert in proofs before taking this class. Fawzi has a way of breaking down concepts in a very simple, easy to follow, and entertaining way that makes his lectures really easy to follow and understand. I feel like as a result of taking 250, I understand proofs way better than after MATH310. Exams: He did complain that he wrote exams that were consistently too easy and mentioned they were going to get harder, but they never got harder, so I’ll take it. His exams consistently have pretty high scores, take mostly lighter content from the lecture, and are basically what you expect from going to lecture. He can be somewhat nitpicky on proof structure, but there’s no math professor out there that DOESN’T nitpick proof structure. One thing I will say is he can sometimes grade things pretty harshly with regards to partial credit. Quizzes: The pop quizzes are the biggest issue with this class for me. They’re mainly a way of keeping people going to discussions, since any given discussion can have a quiz, so you have to go to all of them. All the quizzes are pretty straightforward, and you should normally score 20/20 on them if you’re keeping up with the material. Don’t be afraid to send a regrade request if your grade is messed up and you have an equivalent answer, but don’t send any begging for partial credit (the same applies to exams). He won’t budge on that. TL;DR: Fantastic lecturer, pretty simple exams, and nice person. Not a fan of pop quizzes in discussion go. I feel like if he recorded lecture and announced quizzes, he’d be rated much higher, but he is 100% worth taking for any CS class.

exams were kinda hard but fawzis my goat i genuinely love his teaching style so much i wish he taught higher level cs classes because i miss his slides and examples and sidetracks about his love life or what diet he was on. just do all of the examples on the slides and you should be fine. i know it is a lot of work but you have to put in effort in this class in general to do well, its not fawzi's fault. homeworks are annoyingly long but they actually also rlly help for exams and anythings excusable for this guy i love him

Listen, I’m not saying Cliff is a bad guy, but my pharmacist is about to go out of business because of him. Here is a draft of that story, punch-up comedian style. Feel free to post this on your socials—it’s got that "relatable academic trauma" vibe that usually does numbers. The Cure for Insomnia (Or: Why My Pharmacist Hates Cliff) I’ve been on prescription-strength melatonin for years, right? My blood is basically 40% cherry-flavored sleep aid. I thought I was a medical mystery—a man whose brain simply refused to log off. Then I met Cliff. Cliff doesn’t give lectures; he performs "auditory anesthesia." His voice isn’t a human voice—it’s the sound of a dial-up modem trying to connect in a rainstorm. It’s the sonic equivalent of watching beige paint dry in slow motion. Last week, I walked into Cliff’s room, he said, "Good morning, class, let's look at the sub-sections of paragraph B," and I was out. I didn’t just nap; I entered a coma so deep I saw my ancestors. My Fitbit actually sent a notification to my emergency contacts because it thought I had passed away. I’ve officially cancelled my prescription. Why pay a $20 co-pay for pills when I can just sit in the back row of Cliff's class for free? He is a biological miracle. If we could bottle his energy, we could solve the world’s stress levels in a weekend, mostly because everyone would be unconscious. The only problem? Anwar. See, I actually want to pass Course 330. Anwar is the problem because he’s actually... engaging? He’s funny, he’s got energy, he makes 330 feel like a Netflix special. I’m sitting there in Anwar’s class, wide awake, learning things, and laughing. It’s disgusting. I’m actually retaining information. So now I have a system: I go to Anwar to pass the degree. I go to Cliff to achieve the deepest REM cycle known to man. If you see me sprinting from Anwar’s room to Cliff’s, don’t worry. I’m just heading to my bedroom.

This class was extremely boring, not well taught, and not useful. Projects were fine. He seems nice as a professor but I could not for the life of me stay focused while he rambles on just pointing at stuff in the code editor in a dry voice. I wouldn't recommend taking this course.

Doesn't really matter if you take Cliff or Anwar for 330 as they coteach (same quizzes, projects, exams, schdule, etc.). If you don't like Cliff's slides/notes, you can see Anwars. If you don't like Cliff's lectures, you can go to Anwar's. I went to both and Cliff is slightly more boring, but I also find the material boring in general. This is easily one of the most organized CS classes at UMD. Everything is very clearly communicated, and the course website is SO useful. Every past quiz and exam is available to study with (as well as their answer keys). The TAs are obviously very close with Cliff and seem to enjoy their job, which definitely makes the class feel sort of homey. My biggest complaint were some exam questions were really more reliant on logic than on understanding of the material. These were the minority, but still. Also, Cliff/Anwar both sent out an email to everyone, accusing "many" people in the class of using AI on projects. They offered the usual "snitch on yourself or we'll sent you to OSC". My problem with this is that they sent this a few weeks before finals, and said they wouldn't report anyone until after finals. So, everyone had the threat of a potential false AI accusation looming over their heads throughout finals. Imo it's unprofessional to punish the entire class instead of just contacting/reporting those individuals directly. Also I knew of no one who was reported, so it's somewhat likely they lied to get people to turn themselves in (since you can't really prove AI usage anyways).

He is genuinely a very kind and approachable professor, and I would definitely recommend taking CMSC330 with him. While the course itself is certainly challenging, he creates a welcoming and supportive learning environment that truly makes a difference. He is patient, warm, and always considerate of his students, which helps make a difficult class feel much more manageable and encouraging.

this class was SO easy because of how practical its content is. i literally got a 101% and probably spent at most a 5 hours a week outside of the class on it. you learn about functional programming and regex which are extremely important concepts no matter what language youre using, and then you get to learn a little about compilers which is fun! cliff at times would start talking about something without saying what we were learning about which was a bit confusing, but everyone got the point eventually. he did put me to sleep a bit, but you can understand him still! i am someone who prefers a more organized teaching style usually, but i enjoyed his more laid back approach for a change tbh. this class is also the complete opposite of 351, if you do the practice exams you will know every single question type that is on the exam/quizzes and there are no curveballs. he was a little slow on releasing the projects, but he made them easier when he was behind so im not complaining.
He's a good professor and a warm person in general. I would recommend him for CMSC330 since I think he's better at teaching than Cliff.

I would put 3.5 stars if I could. Cliff is a very average professor. His lectures are boring and long, but that's most CS professors tbh. I can't think of anything he does that's terrible, but I also can't think of anything he does that's especially noteworthy. If you just pay attention during lectures and discussions, you'll be fine.

Not good at teaching and very good at making simple things hard and hard things impossible. Take Anwar if you can. If you must take him, either go to Anwar's lectures & study/review based on his notes or go to Cliff's lectures and risk annoying the entire class by asking questions every 10 minutes because he can't teach without confusing people like so.

Do not recommend Cliff at all. His lectures are extremely unhelpful and he has a cocky attitude when speaking with students. Also, most of his TAs (besides a couple that were really helpful) never answer student questions on piazza and hold useless discussions. I strongly suggest taking Anwar's section instead. Not only is he a better lecturer, he seems to care more about his students.

This class is probably the worst out of all the lower level CS classes in its current state being ran by Mamat and Cliff. It is ran very unprofessionally and poorly. Dr. Mamat, despite being a funny guy, was a horrible lecturer. He would complain that less people came to class but his lectures were genuinely so hard to follow. He posts lecture videos though which you can rewatch and there are Cliff's notes, so you can still do a decent amount of learning on your own. The previous exam solutions had a lot of errors which never got fixed, and you needed to check on piazza to see if someone had clarified the solutions were wrong for a specific question, which was annoying. It was also annoying when we randomly started doing rust with 2 weeks left to go in the semester, and the specifications for the final exam were "anything mentioned in any lecture/discussion/quiz/project up until the last day of class." Overall it's definitely not impossible to do well in this class, it's just ran very poorly which makes it frustrating at times.

Anwar is pretty good. Him and Cliff coteach so no difference as far as exams, projects, etc. He teaches really well imo when he goes off his slides, which is about half the time. And then the other half he just does random examples on emacs which can sometimes be helpful and other times just makes things confusing. But overall he is pretty solid.

The class honestly isn't too difficult content wise. Ocaml coding can be a slightly annoying (especially with lexer, parser, interpreter) and the rust rules may be difficult to understand at first but in the grand scheme of the course, nothing is that hard to pick up. I know many people say Anwar is an entertaining lecturer, but I went to almost every class and struggled to pay attention in every single one. Luckily he started giving out attendance extra credit but as far as what he actually taught, it was awfully boring for me and I found myself doing homework for other classes. I basically taught myself all of the content while studying for the exam or doing the projects. You could honestly chose either Cliff or Anwar for this course but it seems like Anwar was more lenient, especially with his extra credit.

Here is the thing, he really just isn't that good of a lecturer. He can be very disorganized, and he can forget to share vital information unless a student explicitly asks. I think his exams/projects were pretty fair. His projects can be a bit tough, but they certainly help you prepare for the exams so make sure to spend time on them.

Look as a person this guy is great he is funny and has good interaction with his students. However, he may be one of the worst lecturers I have had in terms of learning comprehension. Most of the slides are not in order to his material and often he transitions into examples that sure are useful but then take up a lot of time. As a result theory is much less covered which is a large issue for a class based heavily in it

Gave good examples during class and in his notes which helped with assignments in the course, especially projects. Lectures are definitely disorganized at times with him jumping around content. But when he is focused, you are able to learn a lot in class plus he is a funny guy. Anwar or Cliff? Doesn't matter. The course is the same no matter what so just choose the lecture time that works best for your schedule.

Very nice, wholesome guy who cracks some jokes during lecture. However, easily one of the worst lecturers I've ever had. Any reviews saying that all he does in class is open up emacs and type up random examples in a sleep-inducing kinda way are completely accurate. There doesn't seem to be any coherent order between the topics he covers during lectures, and he absolutely does not follow the corresponding lecture slides. For some topics, he covers them a week or more later than when they appear on the lecture quizzes (which are assigned Friday and due Sunday?). One big issue I have is that Anwar doesn't explain some terms at all. After going to discussion, I realized the topics are very simple and could easily be explained in like 3 seconds. All this being said, 330 has a ton of old quizzes/exams, notes, and resources to reference. It should still be easy to get a good grade if you put effort into the projects and pay attention in discussion.

To be honest, I did NOT like Cliff as a professor whatsoever. First lecture was good, then it went downhill from there. All Cliff does is yap his mind away and it is very hard to pay attention in lectures because he has a very monotone voice and very boring slides. At some point, I stopped going to lecture, and even if I did, I would NOT pay attention. By the middle/end of the semester, there were only 50 kids in the lecture hall. His slides are alright, but tbh, Anwar's material helped me learn the material better. Looking back, the concepts in 330 are not bad whatsoever, just that Ocaml is so annoying to write. He has quizzes that were aight (first one was atrocious, avg was a 9/20). His exams were also pretty straightword, not many curveballs other than putting brainrot on questions that made the question so much harder to understand and solve. PROJECTS 3-5 ARE BAD, START EARLY. For these projects, he made them longer/harder because of cheating in previous semesters. Tbh, I might recommend anwar over cliff but it really doesnt matter.

Cliff has a lack of professionalism that radiates throughout this entire class. He doesn’t hold his TAs to any standards so they often show up late and are unhelpful during discussions and office hours. He tries to be goofy by putting bizarre “brain rot” style questions on midterms, which obviously makes the questions way harder to comprehend. He also never responds to emails. On top of all that, he’s not a great lecturer. He’s not the Devil. I don’t want to be too harsh. But I struggle to come up with any positive contribution that Cliff made to this course that I can’t also attribute to Anwar. I would recommend taking Anwar instead.

Not sure if Anwar is the one who does this, but I hate the structure and organization of this class. The only practice tools are old exams and quizzes which most where in a different curriculum and language entirely. The questions on old quizzes are also significantly different than the quiz I took. There are quizzes randomly assigned Friday close to midnight and due Sunday without any warning. Everything is on only on gradescope and nothing is on elms.

Good teacher overall. He's a boring lecturer imo but you can easily just not go to class because his pdf notes are very well-constructed and cover everything you need. OCaml is tough, and projects 3-5 are the hardest I've had in college so far. Thankfully, only 10-20% of each exam is OCaml coding because 330 is really just a "intro to compilers" course and the theoretical/non-coding topics are pretty easy. Rust is fine. Choosing between him and Anwar doesn't matter. They share exams, projects, quizzes, and the course website, so just pick the better time.

Good professor. Quite literally no difference between him and Cliff. Though, Anwar, being more senior, might be slightly better at teaching. The exams for both professors are more or less the same. In fact, it's pretty evident that Cliff and/or the TAs make the exams given the incorporation of gen z humor. Regardless, the exams are fairly easy, and the only way you fail is if you don't actually do the projects (i.e. don't GPT projects). Both professors' resources are accessible. In fact, most of the resources can be accessed without being enrolled in the class, just search up UMD CMSC 330.

He's not that great of a lecturer, but he's my favorite professor. He's just the best, but not the best at teaching. He's funny too :). Class in general is easy, the projects take a little while, so I'd to starting at least 2 days before the due date. It's annoying to have a project due soon when the autograder does not work right and the readme has 500 typos. This isn't Prof. Mamat's fault, though, I'm pretty sure it's Cliff's fault. The TA's were actually good unlike CMSC351.

Cliff and Anwar typically are the only people who teach this. Material is the same but definitely take Anwar over Cliff. Cliff's lectures are about as boring as can be. Imagine a theatre kid mixed with a introverted CS nerd. I would have rather watched paint dry than attend another one of his lectures as it would've been more entertaining and helpful than his lectures. His notes and slides are a mess filled with snazzy little transitions that make it a nightmare to navigate. The class overall felt pretty unstructured with random topics thrown in for the s and g's. At least Anwar recorded his lectures and explained things better than Cliff's philosophical mumbling. Material is difficult but certainly doable. Start project 5 early. TA's were hit or miss. I think there were maybe one or two good ones but the rest seemed to be pretty rude, unhelpful, or overall useless. My discussion TA was late half the time and spent most of the time arguing with one student about different ways to do problems. That's all I remember until I stopped showing up to the discussion at all.

TLDR, if you have a choice between Anwar of Cliff, take Anwar (better overall lectures and more do able assessments). Cliff personally made this course much harder than it was supposed to be. I usually ended up reading Anwar's slides and examples, and eventually, watch his recordings instead to understand some weird concepts like lambda calculus. Cliff notes made it insanely harder to understand and grasp it in my opinion. The way he designed his slides is just so not intuitive at all, and makes it even harder when he doesn't record his lessons. He has philosopher style of teaching, which makes sense because he got a degree in that. But honestly, teaching cs in that kind of style just makes it harder and more complicated than it's supposed to be for students to understand this class. And remember, 330 is a required class as a cs major, so yeah this matter should be taken seriously rather than a joke. Now yes, keep in mind, 330 is a hard class (it's a 300 level course duh). So yeah regardless it wasn't going to be an easy class anyway. But I just felt like this course was insanely unstructured. They taught things too fast and concepts just felt out of place to grasp personally, especially when it's expected that many students are also taking this class with 351. I also felt like the projects were way too complicated and just weren't helpful in understanding the concepts that we learned in the course, especially project 5. And I also think we could've just learned Rust for the entire course rather than jumping into a new language after already a difficult language (OCaml) weeks before our final. Also, I despised the fact that the course used OCaml to teach us the concepts, honestly made things worse to click and understand... I just felt like if they assessed things with more structure, and actually know what kind of questions to ask on the exams, the course wouldn't have been to hard. Because honestly, the concepts were actually darn easy to grasp, and some of them were pretty cool and essential too (e.g. NFAs/DFAs).
Only complaint is that he tends to mumble sometimes, so I would recommend sitting near the front so you can hear everything he says. Solid lecturer. Makes the topics about as engaging as they can be.

Anwar is nice, but I really hate his style of teaching since it’s so disengaging. Literally on the first day of class, bro just opened up Emacs and started coding random stuff, and he doesn’t reference his slides at all—or barely does. It’s so hard to take notes or follow along with what he’s saying when he just codes for 1 hour and 15 minutes straight. Towards the end of February, I started skipping lecture since he unnecessarily went way too in-depth into the concepts and didn’t teach them in the way they actually show up on the quizzes and exams. This class is nice though because the projects make up a big portion of your grade, and the exams aren’t worth that much. I literally failed the first two exams and did well on the final, and I’m ending with an A- (that’s telling you how easy it is to do well in this class if you lock in on projects). Quizzes are each only 2% of your grade, but they add up so try to do well on them. You just need to study 2–3 days in advance and do the practice, and you’ll be fine. The TAs are way more involved in this course compared to other CS classes, but some of them clearly don’t know anything about the concepts. Also, some are super unhelpful on Piazza, not directly answering questions but posting a link to something else because they're too lazy to answer LMAO. But luckily my discussion TA was super smart, so he was very helpful. Overall, this class won’t be too bad, but try to study in advance and get ahead if you can to make it easier on yourself.

So you came here to decide whether you should take 330 with Cliff or Anwar. Well I'm here to tell you that you should just choose one that has a better schedule for you. In terms of course structure, the projects are the most demanding part of this course. Especially project 5 (you'll see it when you get there). I know this is said in every CS course review ever, but I cannot emphasize this enough when I say that you should start these projects THE DAY they are assigned. Now I will say there were some people who would start the projects with 2-3 days before due date (most people have other overlapping courses like 351) so they can be a bit constrained for time, but still would get it done somehow. However, like I said, the projects are the most important source of learning in the course. Rushing to do them just to pass the test cases is very counterproductive. Use the projects to master the concepts you learn in class (I did NOT do this, hypocrite much??Shh keep reading) and you will see that the exams and quizzes are SO much easier. Any sort of "coding" question you get in a quiz or exam is some permutation of a function you implemented for a relevant project. So if you're not a GPT warrior and actually implemented the functions by understanding what its asking for, those questions should be a breeze. I'll be the one to say it...OCaml is really not that bad to understand (cry about it yall just work with any code that isn't written like a pseudocode). The syntax is really trivial to grasp especially if you have worked with "let" expressions in JS. HOWEVER, the functional programming aspect of it is where it gets tricky. But once you have enough practice with the first two projects (really only the first project because it is designed to teach you how functional programming in OCaml works), you will spend less time worrying about the language itself rather implementing the necessary concepts into the projects. You will *lightly* go over Rust programming towards the last 20% of the course which is a much more traditional language, and is primarily used to teach concepts on type safety. All in all, it is very manageable. I'm still salty that I didn't perform up to my potential in this course because I felt like I really understood what was taught but sold on the exams because I'm a nervous wreck and tend to blank out during them. Use the practice they give you (exams and quizzes dating back to the days where CS wasn't a 40,863 student per class major) and redo them till you can recognize the type of questions on the exam and can get instantly started on solving them. Also, Friday discussions were not it because wtf but they're worth it if you need the additional practice and sometimes you have to go because they give quizzes (they'll tell you when they are so don't worry). So go to discussion. Or don't.

I didn’t have the best experience with Dr. Mamat in CMSC330. While he clearly knows the material well, I found his lectures to be hard to follow and often disorganized. First, I'm just going to start by saying, for CMSC330, it really doesn't matter much who your professor is. The lecture quizzes, projects, discussion content, TAs, quizzes, and exam content are all exactly the same. The only difference between Cliff and Anwar's sections are which professor's lecture you can attend, but even then, you can just not go to lecture or go to the other professor's lecture (some people found that they didn't really like Cliff's lectures and attended Anwar's instead---the class is big enough that I don't think people would notice). Unlike CMSC351, the professor doesn't make that much of a difference for this class, so don't freak out too much. That being said, I do think Dr. Mamat has the better slides and also more experience in the field, so if you do have a choice, I would probably choose him. Here are my comments on the course in general (this applies for both Dr. Mamat and Cliff's sections): - All the class information is available here: https://bakalian.cs.umd.edu/330 - Literally 44% of your grade is free. Why do I say this? Projects make up 40% of your grade and you get around two weeks for each of them (give or take). You can ask questions, ask the TAs, look up documentation, submit as many times as you want, etc. within those two weeks. Each project took me between two and eight hours. There is literally no reason not to get 100% on every project as long as you don't procrastinate. The median on every project was between 97% to 100%. So again, there's literally no reason not to get 100% on every project, and they make up 40% of your grade. - 4% of your grade is lecture quizzes, which are completely open internet. You can run the code on your own machine, and the lowest couple of lecture quizzes get dropped. So between the lecture quizzes and the projects, 44% of your grade should be completely free (as long as you don't procrastinate). - The quiz medians were: Quiz 1: 14.5/20, Quiz 2: 15/20, Quiz 3: 17.25/20, Quiz 4: 16/20. You get access to all of the course's quizzes and solutions from previous semesters, which is helpful for studying. I honestly found the quizzes to be harder than the midterms. I will say, though, that I didn't do very well on the first two quizzes, but after that, I started buckling down and really studying and I got 100s on the last two quizzes. You'll be fine as long as you study and really understand the content. - 46% of your grade is two midterms and the final. The medians were: Midterm 1: 83, Midterm 2: 87.5, Final (with extra credit): 89.48. Just like the quizzes, you get access to every previous midterm and their answer key, and around ~85% of the content on these exams is the same every semester. They do change things up a bit sometimes (like I think we did more garbage collection than they did in previous semesters), but most of the exams cover very similar content to previous semesters, so it's really helpful to take previous exams for practice. And yes, there's extra credit too. - The discussions were fine. I didn't find them too helpful. There were good review questions, but they were all in a publicly-available GitHub repository that you could look at without going to discussion. Honestly, unless you're really confused about something, you can usually just look up your question online or on Piazza. There are a gazillion resources out there for Ocaml and Rust. For Cliff in particular, I thought his lectures are okay. He goes at a very manageable pace; definitely not fast by any means. He can get pretty theoretical sometimes. He answers questions well. TLDR; This is a fairly easy class as long as you don't procrastinate on your projects and do previous quizzes/exams as practice. I would recommend not starting the projects any later than the weekend before they're due. You could definitely knock them out over the weekend (if you don't mind working on the weekends). I thought the content was fairly interesting, although obviously that's extremely subjective and is up to how interested you are in this type of stuff. I would recommend taking Dr. Mamat but it really doesn't matter that much.
He expects you to watch pre-recored lectures as well as going to class.

This class is easy if you rather learn it yourself, otherwise avoid it like the plague. The tests are actually pretty easy because the questions are very similar to the hw he gives. Obviously avoid this man like the plague if you can. BUT, if you're stuck taking him, understand that you will not learn anything whatsoever. There is literally zero point in expecting to actually get better at algorithms after taking 351 with Kruskal. Lectures are completely laughable. Better off not attending them tbh. TAs are... there. I mean, go say hi, little chit-chat. Not much actual help though. You learn quickly that this class is one of the more unorthodox "sink-or-swim" experiments, if we can call it that. Btw, don't expect any semblance punctuality with grading whatsoever. Also the class is very exam-heavy. And that too only 3 exams that make up 83% of your grade. That's very stressful. And the Homeworks aren't graded that leniently either. No place for free points. And make sure you do as well as you can on the midterms because the final is just brutal. We got back our second midterm grades a week before the final. Also half the grades (as far back as grades before the first midterm took place) wasn't even graded one week before the exam. No point stressing, just chill. Best way to study for the exams is to a) Understand all the past exam and practice exam material, and the homework problems and try think of different spins on them, different variations they can be asked in, etc. This helps SO much. b) Understand the algorithms and all topics covered on the exams extremely well conceptually. c) Memorize, and then change up and iterate whatever practice exam material questions provided. If you do these things, the exams are quite frankly not extremely hard. Just not enough time to answer everything with your best attempt. Honestly, other than the final I kinda winged the exams, maybe spent a day or so studying for them. What I did not do was care to reference any of the lectures or try and learn things on the go. There are maybe 1 or 2 questions that require you to use your intuition and reason quickly. But otherwise, the exams are just practice exam and homework questions reworded or asked with some kind of twist. The optimal approach is to answer those quickly first, and then spend a lot of time to reason out the 1 or 2 thinking-heavy questions The exams are graded extremely harshly. Again, you'll get so many points off even when you are conceptually correct because of some little thing. You HAVE to push regrade requests after every exam. Otherwise you leave a ton of points on the table. You'll still probably do terrible on the exams. But so will everyone else. Our exam averages were 57, 58, and 43. So make of that what you will. The one saving grace of this class is the all ole mighty curve. For this Semester: A+: 85+, A: 75+, A-: 70+, B+: 65+, B: 60+, B-: 55+, C+: 51+, C: 46+, C-: 41+. I **thought** I did much better on the final than I actually ended up scoring. Grading, like I mentioned before, ridiculous how ridiculous it is. Just farm regrade requests if you answered a question mostly correctly but got all or most points off. One thing though was that I ended up being surprised by the relative generosity of the curve, so there's that. So for that reason and that reason alone, I'm giving this 2 stars rather than 1.
Don't do it to yourself... Just wait the semester out until a different professor can be taken. Never seen such low averages in my life. 41% was a C- btw...

I found his personality very interesting but dear god where his exams the worst exams I have ever taken. 85% was dictated on 1-2 midterms and a final which aimed to ask a handful of complex problems instead of a lot of smaller ones. I took him for 351 which is all about algorithms and efficiency but this meant that if you just didn't have a good idea for an algorithm to tackle the question he asks then that is 20 points off the exam. There is a shaky relationship with partial credit since the TAs are grading off of his pretty strict rubric that demands a baseline level of efficiency otherwise you receive no credit. This practice doesn't pair well with his very unique classroom strategy of "class is optional 90% of the time but you have to watch a lecture I recorded years ago and answer a few questions about it to learn the material and we will discuss that in class." TLDR: If you are not a very outgoing/proactive student (similar to me) this class will most likely be tough. P.S. the mean for all three exams we took was ~55 with a standard deviation of ~20. He curved it based on standard deviation from the mean which means a 41 passes with a C-.

As the last review says, this is also my first time writing a review (that says enough already). This is by far the most disorganized, poorly structured, and poorly taught class I have ever taken by far. If you've walked by Kruskal's office in Iribe, that about says enough. Now for some perhaps, a structure where you learn from poorly recorded videos and irrelevant in-person lectures is just a blast, where you don't know midterm and final topics until 5 days before the exams. Comparing this to taking CMSC330 simultaneously, this is a complete 180, where that class has plenty of practice material, notes and lecture recordings available, etc. You have nothing to study for on exams, expect practically needing to memorize all the relevant recorded lectures, as well as having a chance at a problem or two appearing from a previous exam a few years back. Note that all you are provided for study material, is a previous exam, and irrelevant practice questions that exist to get you to think critically, and that's about it. Even then, 1/3 of the questions you will be faced, are "thinking" problems that have little relevance to anything you've learned. Quizzes are due the morning of the in person lecture, and often aren't announced or posted until the evening of the day before, so god forbid you're studying or doing work for another class, you have no choice but to focus on that first. In terms of the TAs, it's almost as if they tried to find the most fitting ones, in correspondence to what the professor is like. The TAs (like the professor himself) never reply to emails, or if you get lucky, one will reply in a week, asking questions in Piazza is also useless, I really don't even know why this class had one. They took forever to grade things, even simple multiple choice quizzes, not to mention not getting your exam feedback returned until the next exam comes around. As for the professor, he is more likely than not on tenure, concerns have been voiced regarding his teaching millions of times, and nothing has been done. You will not even get a GLANCE at what your grade is in the class, til grades are due (note that your final and other quizzes are still not graded, that's why I said a glance). You will spend your whole semester biting your nails and ripping your hair out, not knowing what the cutoff will be like, how you're doing in the class, and what curveball will be thrown at you next. If that isn't enough, grades were due on Dec. 23, and get ready for this...ITS THE NEXT YEAR and there is still no grade in Testudo. It's important to add, that Kruskal grades on a curve, so when you see the class averaging 50 on exams, I wouldn't think too much of it, as I'll provide the cutoffs below. I don't understand how this could be the best way to go about grading for this class. Why not create manageable problems, curriculum, exams, etc. and grade on a more normal scale, perhaps adjusting cutoffs, like all the other CS classes do? Note that as far as I understand it, as long as you land within the standard deviation on exams, given your quiz and homework grades are good enough, you will pass. I certainly have a lot more on my mind that I've gathered throughout the semester, but for the reader's purposes I will end it off on some tips and class information. Tips: -Memorize those lecture recordings like there's no tomorrow. This is practically a self-learning course, and this is about 99% of your learning resources. -Do the Homeworks yourself! Only once finished check with your friend or ChatGPT. Exams will consist of one or two reworded homework problems. -In-person lectures are mostly a waste of time, unless you're genuinely curious about the irrelevant information provided to you, so use that time to study instead. -Your best practice material will be a previous year's exam and your homeworks, and those consist of 50 to 66% of the exam -For the "thinking" problems, try to get some practice online or from Leetcode, based on what the "thinking" problem is like from the previous exam -Only rely on all of the above if you were not able to get a spot in Justin's class, but I would do everything in my ability to go back and take his class instead. Before I provide grading metrics, I want to summarize that this is a counter-intuitive, self-learning course, with an elderly hard of hearing professor, who doesn't plan on changing or modifying the structure of his course whatsoever. I am by any means not a morning person, but I do consider myself to be organized, so if sacrificing those early hours of sleep and not losing my ability to sleep is available (along with receiving a better education), choose Justin, and do everything in your power to do so. Last but not least, even with the tips provided above, you will still often encounter problems on exams that are nothing like what you studied for, and it's not uncommon to encounter a topic which lacks fundamental resources to even know what to expect from it. Either start meditating or choose a faith, because that's all that can protect your mental state here. https://www.cs.umd.edu/classconcern Cutoffs for THIS semester: -A+: 85.0 - 100, A: 75.0 - 84.99, A-: 70.0 - 74.99, B+: 65.0 - 69.99, B: 60.0 - 64.99, B-: 55.0 - 59.9, C+: 51.0 - 54.99, C: 46.0 - 50.99, C-: 41.0 - 45.99 Exam Statistics THIS semester: -Midterm 1: Avg: 56.6, Std Dev: 22 -Midterm 2: Avg: 58.5, Std Dev: 25.94 -Final: Avg: 56.61, Std Dev: 16.53 Grading Percentages THIS semester: 25% — Midterm 1, 25% — Midterm 2, 33% — Final exam, 7% — Regular Homeworks, 4% — NP Homeworks, 6% —Quizzes -6 lowest quizzes were dropped Good luck.

I think this is the last time Justin is teaching 351 in-semester for a while, but certainly take him when you can. I'll be taking him for 420. We shall see how the 7-exam system works, but 351 is as of writing the more traditional 3 50-minute exams and a final which are 83.33% (500 of 600 points) of the course. Pros: - He's done this so many times that he can guide everyone from start to end of the topics and the course without many hiccups. - The entire official class notes are already posted, and he just rewrites a section of them for each lecture. - Exams are straighforward, follow the 3ish practice exams you get. They're always the same difficulty level, no curveballs. - You'll lose a few points on the homeworks here and there but they're generally helpful for understanding stuff. - Great guy living a great life. If you need anything, he'll help where he can. - Can't emphasize enough how nice it is to not have shenanigans going on in a class. No games to be played, just learn content however you do best, do homeworks, take exams, repeat. Cons? - Really the only downside that I could imagine is that he keeps having to simplify the course, so we go slower than we could and don't learn as much as we used to. It's cool stuff, and I wouldn't mind learning more of it. - I guess I should mention 351 has no discussion (didn't see a need for one), and Justin doesn't do recordings, which again there's daily handwritten notes and the permanent full body of LaTeX notes. For me, it was worth getting up at 6am to commute and go to all the 8am lectures. Prioritize getting a seat in a Justin section.
