He is the sole reason why I willed myself to class every week. I am truly appreciative of him and his work throughout this semester, and highly recommend him not only as a TA, but as a role model.

He is an excellent professor who puts genuine effort into explaining algorithmic concepts clearly and provides decent learning resources. His lectures are engaging, he communicates effectively and maintains the class’s attention without being monotonous. However, his exams and homework assignments can be quite difficult, and his grading tends to be strict. He places strong emphasis on understanding and accurately tracing his pseudocode for algorithms. Overall, the course is challenging, striking a balance that makes it a rewarding learning experience.

This is my first time ever writing a review, but DO NOT TAKE HIS CLASS. You will find out midterm topics a week before the exam, if he even tells you when they are. Get ready to just learn through super old videos, but if you can manage learning through just those you should be okay. But no he's actually the worst so avoid at all costs.
Definitely overrated. 5/10 lecturer with good notes but I really don't understand how his rating is so high. Maybe it's more of a relative rating because most other CS professors are abhorrent.

I had ting for 351 but I willingly woke up to come to this class at 8am and it was so worth it. Justin is a GOAT and so are his notes. They are very easy to understand and helped me tremendously. Even if you do not go to class, if you just are up tot date on his notes, then you will be fine!

Ting is a very nice and kind prof and she really cares about her students. HOWEVER, her lectures are atrocious. She just reads of slides and writes over them with her mousepad so it is very messy. Additionally, her accent doesn't help either, so going to lecture and trying to pay attention is so hard. If you have Ting with 351 and Justin(the goat) is teaching, do yourself a favor and look at his notes and go to his lectures. I only went to tings lecture on the first day, exam review, and exam days. Moving on to exams, you will want to take her (if she is coteaching with justin) because her exams were easier than justins and more straightforward, no curveballs, and she grades leniently. Even for homework, I forgot to submit something once and she let me submit it late (whereas justin didnt let my friend submit late). Overall, she is a very nice and caring person but her lectures are not it whatsoever.

She’s a great person, and it’s clear that she really wants all her students to succeed, but her lecturing abilities are atrocious. If she co-teaches with Justin, you’ll be fine as long as you go over his notes or attend his lectures. If she isn’t co-teaching, I’d look for other options.

Justin is probably a good instructor and the course itself is fair and well-run, but he teaches summer 351 like a student urinates in his coffee every morning and he can't stop it. He spent the entire summer crashing out over apparent AI usage and threatening half the class with XF's. These weekly keyboard warrior Piazza posts were really annoying and caused stress for everyone. Bro started glazing corporate layoffs as a good thing at some point. Anyways, if you tune out the meltdowns the course is very manageable. For Justin's sake maybe he should try in-person exams...

As I am writing this, Justin's average rating on Planet Terp is a 4.69, which is even higher than Christopher Kauffman's average rating (4.63). I had Kauffman for CMSC216 in Spring 2025, and he was one of my favorite professors of all time. Needless to say, given all the hype around Justin, I had high hopes going into his Summer 2025 CMSC351 class. Now that everything's over, I can say in short that I am disappointed with my experience. To be clear, Algorithms as a Computer Science class is commonly agreed to be a terrible class pretty much everywhere (based on talking to many people from many different universities). A large part of this is due to the fact that those with minimal experience in algorithmic programming find the class extremely difficult, while at the same time those with a lot of experience in algorithmic programming (i.e. competitive programmers, like myself) often dislike how it is taught very mathematically rigorously yet with little intuition or application at all (in other words, this class will not get you through a coding interview). Yes, Algorithms, the Computer Science class, is more of a math class than anything else. Yes, Computer Science is not just programming, and Theoretical Computer Science consists almost entirely of pure math. All of this I knew going in. None of this prepared me for when I lost over half the points on a problem from Homework 1 asking me to prove the Big Omega of a certain function. I approached it as almost everyone intuitively would; like almost everyone had been taught to solve an inequality since middle and high school. Set up the inequality. Turn it into an equality, and solve the equality. Determine which side of the solution to the equality contains the solutions to the inequality, and turn the solution to the equality back into an inequality. Apparently, this wasn't rigorous enough for this class. Perhaps this is just how this kind of stuff works. But we were never told that solving it this way would lose us points, and the "correct" way shown in lecture seemed unnecessary and unintuitive. Instead, Justin complained that many submitted solutions to such Big-Omega or Big-O proof problems looked the way AI would solve them, and that such proofs "were generally terrible", citing several reasons why. Okay, fine. If you take Justin, make sure you explain the hell out of any answer you give to a problem. One problem on Exam 1 asked me to find a lower bound for something and to show my work. I later received minimal points for my answer, despite the fact that the work I had shown lined up almost exactly with a potential full-score answer as described in the rubric. Upon submitting a regrade request, I was told the grader had no idea what I did here at all, since I had no explanation at all. But the question never asked me to explain! Too bad. There was an Exam 1 MCQ problem which asked whether a certain algorithm was O(N^2) in the best case. The algorithm in question was O(N) in the best case and O(N^2) in the worst case. As such, most people in the class answered incorrectly, as technically an algorithm which is O(N) is also O(N^2). A big stink was made on Piazza about this being a trick question hidden in a sea of 50 multiple-choice questions, designed to make students lose points even if they understood the concepts it was supposed to be testing. One student asked, to avoid confusion, why didn't Justin instead ask if the algorithm was O(N^100) in the best case? Another student literally included a screenshot of the Merriam-Webster entry for "trick question" in their post. In the end, Justin's response was that technically the algorithm was indeed O(N^2) in the best case, and that he didn't expect his students to autopilot any questions in his class. On top of this, there was a question on the Exam 2 MCQ which, depending on your interpretation of the question (the wording was not specific enough), could have had two different answers. However, if you picked the wrong one, you had no recourse, not even through Piazza. By this point, people had long since stopped posting on Piazza about questions like this. They knew nothing would result from their efforts. On Exam 3, there was a free-response question which essentially asked us to find the shortest path between two points in "a simple, connected graph" (I'm simplifying greatly). Many test takers realized quickly the looseness of this wording and realized that, to cover all graphs which could potentially be described as "a simple connected graph", one needed to run Floyd's Algorithm, as a simple BFS would not cover the case of a weighted graph, and Dijkstra's would not cover the case of negative-weight edges. Once Exam 3 was graded, everyone who wrote such a solution to that problem lost points (there was a special "tier" in the Gradescope "rubric" for Floyd/Dijkstra solutions). I asked in my regrade request what the issue with my answer was. The response was that I needed to specify what my input to Floyd's Algorithm was or what graph I was going to run it on. I responded that in my answer I had specified running Floyd's algorithm on precisely the graph described in the problem statement. The response? "It does not define distances". Confused and a little frustrated, I wandered over to take a look at the official Exam 3 solutions. The official solution to that problem? Run a BFS. Justin is not the worst professor I've ever had (currently, that (dis)honor goes to Manoussos Grillakis). Justin is not the devil. Justin is not someone who you need to avoid at all costs. But as a student in his Summer 2025 CMSC351 class, I felt like the environment I was in was just a little bit more hostile than I was used to. Every time I made a Piazza post, I was just a little bit nervous that I'd get flamed by Justin in his response. I felt a severe lack of benefit of the doubt, almost to the point of actively trying to take away points from correct or mostly correct answers. Look. Everyone's different, and everyone is looking for different things in the professors they get. Lord knows I have some unpopular opinions regarding some UMD professors (both positively and negatively). You might enjoy Justin when you take him, or he might even have just been going through something this summer. All I'm saying is, if you do decide to take him (and, to be honest, given that the alternative is Kruskal, you might decide to take him anyway): don't say I didn't warn you.

I don't really see the hype. Don't get me wrong, he is good, but so is Fawzi. On pure lecturing, Fawzi is actually better, but overall interaction with students in office hours and stuff like that, I'll give that to Justin. There are a couple of other Lecturers too. The hype for Justin is RIDICULOUS, lol. The class is heavily weighted on Exams. You make a small mistake, you lose a lot of points -> you get a bad grade in the class. That's not Justin's fault, I guess, since the class is a theory course. Justin made A LOT of errors on the midterms. How's that for the GOAT teacher, huh? What a joke. Oh I almost forgot, lectures being 8 AM, I wish he recorded the lectures. Well, bro wakes up at 4 AM everyday so. Just take Ting.
If she is co-teaching with Justin again -- 5/5. Her exams are more fair, and she is a very nice person and a lenient grader. Just study off of Justin's notes and do well on the midterms

Do not take Kruskal unless you 100% for certain have to. If you have to, heres what to expect - Cons: You will not get a syllabus until two weeks in. You will not know what date your midterms are until a week before. You not be given study material until a few days before an exam. The study material you do get will not be like the exam at all (do previous semesters exams instead, those are good). Your assignments will not be graded in time for midterms and the final, so you wont know if you are doing well or not. You will spend more time trying to figure out what the homework is asking you to do than actually doing it. The exams consist of reworded homework questions + "thinking" problems that you have not seen before, and it is up to your intellect and intuition to solve it. You learn via pre-made lecture videos, some are good, some are bad. In-person lectures are basically just discussions where you go over quizzes and he talks about irrelevant topics that wont be on the exam. Pros: You will do bad in this class, but so will everyone else. The curve is generous. This semester, the cutoffs were A+: 86+ A: 77-86 A-: 71.5-77 B+: 67.5-71.5 B: 63-67.5 B-: 59-63 C+: 55.5-59 C: 52-55.5 C-: 48.5-52 D: 35-48.5. Statistically you'll probably be fine in the end, but you wont enjoy doing it.

I found Ting's lectures very hard to follow. The slides presented information in a sort of confusing way, and her poor handwriting and strong accent made it a little hard for me to understand what was going on when we were getting into more difficult concepts. I ended up mostly using Justin's class notes and online resources because they were much easier to learn from. Since they co-teach, this worked out fine for homeworks and exams. If you, like me, are not a morning person, you are probably better off with Ting.

Terrible professor. He doesn't care about the class at all; he always came to class late, he never graded in a timely manner, when he released solutions to homework and practice exams they were almost always incomplete, and more than a few times outright incorrect. When answering questions, both in person and online, his answers are typically vague and unhelpful (he is also hard of hearing, so it will take some effort and some help from the people in the front row to get his attention). Going to his class is useless most of the time, just go do the homework instead. His lectures didn't contain testable material until the last few weeks, and he always fumbled through them, as though he only half remembered the material. I think it's likely that he made his slides years ago and only looks at them during class when he has to deliver them. All the material that appeared on homework and exams (until like three weeks from the end) was given through video lectures. There were two kinds of these. The first was videos he recorded at home during the pandemic, and these are actually quite good. It's very useful to see his process when trying to replicate it on the homework. The second type was recordings taken of him delivering the lecture in a normal class, and these are much worse. The sound quality is terrible, there's a ton of awkward silences, he spends a lot of time answering questions from students who you can't hear, and they take about twice as long as the first type to cover the same amount of material. The homework is hard, but not as hard as understanding what it wants you to do in the first place. Logistically the entire class was a nightmare. He didn't give us a syllabus until the second week of classes. He made his midterms two hours long, which required booking different rooms to hold them in, which took him a ton of time to make work, which meant that we didn't learn the actual dates of the midterms until the week before. By the end of the semester he was well over a month behind on grades. Final grades were due on Friday, May 23rd, and we got them on Tuesday, May 27th. The exams are heavily based on the homework, usually copypasting the hardest problem from a recent homework and changing the numbers. Reminder again that they are two hours long. Just leaving this here: https://www.cs.umd.edu/classconcern Avoid at all costs.

If you aren't a morning person, then it's best to take Ting over Justin. Justin typically teaches his only 351 section at 8 am, so if you believe you'll be mentally awake to take midterms that early in the day, then take him. However, if you're not a morning person at all, then you should definitely take Ting over Justin, especially since the midterms are very similar (Ting's are only very slightly harder). Other than that, the homeworks are the same, you get access to Justin's notes (which are enough to learn from), and the final for both professors is the same. Also, it's not that hard to follow lectures (you honestly don't even need to go to them) in spite of Ting's accents.

He is an okay lecturer; he's somewhat interesting to listen to, although I think the lectures are still a bit boring. He teaches the topics well and makes complicated algorithms easy to understand. I would've given him 4 stars if it weren't for his tests. I have never been in a class where I understood the topics but received a bad grade because of so many lazy mistakes in writing the exam. Although I did score poorly on exam 1 and the final, I understand it was my abilities and not the professor's or the exam's fault. Exams 2 and 3 had 2 major issues. Exam 2 had a question saying simplify that was some ludicrous summation, and I spent the majority of the whole exam trying to figure this out, leaving me with no time for the remaining questions. A TA specifically said a summation could not be in the answer as well. When exam grades came out, the rubric had a summation as the answer. He did give us the points if we had the summation in our work, but wasting my time on this led me to make stupid mistakes on others I would've caught if I had time to check. The question also gave us initial conditions that made no sense for the question. Exam 3 had a question that was literally impossible, which was clarified at the end after having spent 15 minutes second-guessing my knowledge. Every exam had a crazy number of issues. Like a question on the FIRST PAGE that wasn't even in the exam material. Although these issues were noted quickly, unlike the other two. I don't understand how these exams are made. One look at it and you can find all these issues, I thought it was standard practice to give the exams for the TAs to take.

Reviews Below are accurate. Amazing lecturer and an even better person to talk to during office hours. Has tons of wisdom and is always willing to lend a helping hand and is there for his students. Homework problems are harder than exams. Just wished I had studied more and probably would have gotten a better grade. Each exam has 1-2 curveball questions which makes sure you really understand the material. Make sure to understand material inside and out and understand pseudocode. Lectures are great but he doesn’t do hard examples. Practice exam material is fair.

Ting is genuinely a nice person and very lenient and flexible. Literally, she just gives you back points if you were able to explain your case against why you think you were right (even if it's not correct), she gives back some points to you anyways during office hours. Now, I will say she is definitely not the best lecturer in any form. I never ended up going to her lectures because she wasn't able to teach properly and just speaks jargon most of the time. What saved me in this course was Justin's Notes (God bless Justin). His notes were just mwah, it helped me understand all the concepts without going to any lectures at all, along with watching YT videos of the one and only, Abdul Bari (the goat). I did pretty decent on Ting's exams, and her exams felt pretty do able to me personally. What I found challenging in this course were the weekly online quizzes as some of the questions were actually wack and difficult to answer, and tanked my grade a bit from that. Additionally, I actually had around an A- initially before the final (including all grades except the final), but I kinda bombed the final, which by the way was 30% of my grade, which brought me down to a B. Overall the course is pretty do able and actually great with Ting. Just study well for the final, unlike me hahaha, and you will do well in the course!

Nice person, okay lecturer. A lot of people say the strategy is to sign up for her sections and attend Justin's lectures instead - you get the better lecturer and better exams. I didn't do that and felt the class was just fine. Some of her explanations for algorithms or concepts were a bit unintuitive, but it clicked for me later after minimal self study. I personally found that Justin's notes were good for overviewing concepts at a high level, while Ting's slides were better for reviewing specific details and the why/how of certain concepts. Again, I didn't attend Justin's lectures, so I don't know if what he says out loud fills in the gaps in what he writes and makes it stick. I found her office hours very helpful. She's willing to put in the time to explain tough stuff and answer stupid questions, she's pretty responsive with emails, and she takes student feedback on adjusting the difficulty of midterms. I feel bad giving her a 3/5 and she'll probably improve (this was her second semester teaching I think?) but I think it's accurate to my experience. All in all, you won't go wrong with her. Just put in the work and you'll be fine.

She co-taught with Justin so I ended up not attending lecture at all and just looking at Justin's lecture notes, and also Justin's lecture videos from Spring 2021. I really liked Ting's exams: There were no curveballs, a lot of partial credit, and if I put in the time to study it wasn't too difficult to get an A on them. I can't speak on her teaching ability as I did not go to any lectures but she seemed super kind and helpful on piazza, emails, and office hours. Her slides were also excellent (Not as good as Justin's notes but that's understandable honestly). Overall, I found this course to be pretty chill and the material in it to be engaging and interesting. I would definitely recommend taking her for 351.

I did not even have Justin for 351 this semester I had ting but I still felt compelled to review this absolute goat of a man. Despite having Ting I attended many Justin lectures this semester and he is without a doubt the best lecturer not only in the CS department but quite possibly across all departments at UMD. Justins lectures do not feel like a lecture, it feels like someone just casually having a conversation with you about the topic being covered but in the best way possible. He can make anyone understand any topic and it's truly a gift that not many people have. His notes are incredible. He posts notes for every lecture that are color coded and have diagrams of different algorithms and you could 100% get away with never showing up to class and just reading those notes, that's how good they are.(not saying to do this) If Justin is an option, take him! I hope to have the privilege of taking another CS class with him during my time at UMD Justin, you are the GOAT

Justin takes notes on his Ipad while explaining the algorithm/time complexity at 8 am. Plz wake up and go he's great. You really need to know all the algorithms gory details to do well on exams so don't slack. It felt like the same amount I would study for calc 2. I learned alot from Justin he's good at math his notes are neat and clear. HomeWorks are challenging but good practice. Make sure for exams your fast cause 50 mins will go by quick. Know these algo's well. Your in good hands

As other students have stated here, Ting is a nice person and seems approachable, and she clearly cares about her students' success. She’s somewhat active on Piazza and provides helpful advice on how to study and succeed in the class (e.g., prioritizing topics for midterms and the final). Unfortunately, the language barrier makes it difficult for her to communicate the material in a way that resonates with everyone. If you're comfortable understanding it, then more power to you. But for more security in your grade, I’d suggest the following approach: Attend Ting's first 2–3 lectures to gauge how well you can follow along. Then, consider attending Justin's lectures (I started around the Big-O/Big-Omega/Big-Theta topics) and see how you find them. Personally, I found Justin's co-teaching more helpful for understanding the material, even though it meant waking up at 8 AM. By the middle of the semester, I stopped attending Justin's lectures in person, instead reviewing his notes when doing the homework. I also used office hours to clarify concepts and help others out, which helped me practice the material. Occasionally, I’d attend Justin’s 8 AMs just to refresh my understanding. In hindsight, I probably should’ve been more consistent with attending Ting’s lectures, as my exam grades weren’t as high as they could’ve been. Grade Breakdown: MCQs: 7.69% Homework: 15.38% Exams: 46.15% Final Exam: 30.78% Total: 100% Most of the MCQs were straightforward, with about 14 in total this semester. You should be able to score 100% on most of them, though the number of MCQs and homework assignments may change each semester, as well as the number that are dropped. Many students, including myself, used a group chat to compare answers, and we attended office hours for clarification on more open-ended questions. While I didn’t go to Ting’s office hours, I’ve heard they’re helpful. The TAs for both Ting and Justin were shared this semester, and there were a few associated with Ting’s sections that I found especially helpful toward the end of the semester. As for Ting's exams, there’s a good amount of partial credit, so even if you make a few mistakes, it’s rare that you’d need to request a regrade. However, exams accounted for roughly 77% of our grade, so even if you perform well on homework and MCQs, you still need to do well on the midterms and final to get an A. If you handle exam pressure well and avoid small mistakes adding up, you should be in a strong position for this class.

Yeah he's a good lecturer and well organized and you'll learn a lot in this class. And I'm not gonna dismiss what other reviews have said about him. But... He can be very cold when it comes to grading. 76% of the class grade relies on exams, for which 3 of them are 50 minute exams. Justin I want you to understand something. Someone can understand a topic very well and could even iterate the ins and outs of it back to you within demand. However, working within a time constraint can usually add a level of stress to students which can cause (especially in a computation heavy course) minor mathematical errors to happen. I have made these mistakes countless times and you still somehow believe that I'm just fighting for points just because. People's livelihoods depend on a single letter you enter at the end of the semester. So not being considerate or telling people "its graded according to the rubric" is just very invalidating of some people's honest hard work. But you do you for sure! But yeah to people looking to take him he's the best option for this course regardless. He will make sure to teach what you need to know, and the rest is up to you.

My role model. Explains difficult concepts super well and an overall amazing person. Lectures were organized and notes were posted within minutes after. Could not recommend Justin more if you have the opportunity to take him.

Ting is a really nice and understanding professor. I had friends who took Justin and he didn't let them submit their homework even a little bit late or retake any quizzes; this one time I forgot about a lecture quiz and I emailed Ting and she was so kind and understanding; she let me redo it without any real explanation. Also she's super young so she's chill like I have literally seen her at the dining hall with her friends. I will say her lectures were really boring and hard to understand at times because of her accent so if you want to learn stuff with organized notes, go to Justin's lecture. I also 100% recommend taking Ting over Justin despite the reviews because, I cannot stress this enough: her exams were SO MUCH EASIER than Justin's. Justin exams always threw some kinda curveballs at you where you really have to understand the core of the concept or some crazy detail in the psuedocode while Ting's questions were mostly straightforward. The cheat code for this class is to source the exams from the previous semester because she reuses a lot of questions so keep that in mind it really helped me practice for the real thing, even though she did supply her own practice exams.
Had a traumatic semester with him last year. Don't take him. All the good reviews here are definitely him writing them.

before i start this review, all the people that say this class isnt that hard or "just get above the avg and you can get an A" as if thats easy is just all BS... maybe its just my sem but literally this was the hardest and most uncoordinated course i have ever taken, even after finals i dont even have assignments graded that was due back in February so I dont even know my grade right now. The exams were very hard but just try your best to stay in the standard dev or avg. An annoying thing was how many people were cheating during the exam which was increasing the average like someone was on chatgpt during the whole exam and the TAs did not even care. DONT GET ME STARTED ON THAT FINAL EXAM... not even joking or exaggerating one bit but that final exam was the hardest exam i have ever taken in my entire life, also kruskal didnt put out final study material until 5 DAYS BEFORE THE FINAL which is insane to me, and even after studying i felt like nothing on the final was remotely close to what we practiced in class. AND HE DOES NOT EVEN GIVE PARTIAL CREDIT so dont expect him to be generous in the grading, unless hes ur absolute LAST OPTION, DO NOT TAKE KRUSKALLLL

I don't even know where to start... He doesn't seem to care about this course in the slightest, showing up 10 min late to every lecture and not having anything prepared. Don't expect to have anything graded either, i put my expected grade as a C- but only 20% of the assignments are graded so far so who knows. Exams were very difficult but not hard to stay within the standard dev. My BIGGEST complaint about this class was the amount of cheating that happened during the exams. People literally had their grok ai SPEAKING to them during the final and the TAs ignored it, they also don't find it suspicious when 10 people were in the bathroom at a time when there were 30 people talking an exam. Kruskal simply does not care. The final exam was the hardest thing i've done academically by far, no material could help you prepare for the final it was %90 intuition based. I had all the concepts down but Kruskal will introduce some random-property graph that you've never seen in your life and expect you to understand it. More than half of the final was stuff we never even practiced in hw or in class, I could do it during the exam because I do leetcode but I would never expect someone with no experience to intuitively solve these problems. And you might think because the questions are so hard the grading on them might be lenient... NO, I wrote down my answer for a question with %100 confidence and I got 0/15 when one of the criteria was +3 points for "something of value" when mind you not, my answer was off by a few coefficients and i had a full explanation. AVOID KRUSKAL at all costs, if I could go back i would swallow my ego and take 351 over the summer with Justin. This semester has been so stressful for a class that shouldn't be too hard.

i was able to follow along with her lectures fairly well but everyone is different, a lot of my friends in the section just went to justin's lecture instead. since she co-teaches with him, you have tons of resources to work with. exams are very fair, very similar to the practice she gives out and there isn't anything crazy, she will tell you exactly what's on it too. i'm giving her five stars because she's super sweet and wants her students to do well, she will probably improve over time

The only reasons I'm giving her 2 stars instead of 1 is because she's nice and she co-teaches with Justin. I'll cut her some slack in saying that there is a language barrier present. However, aside from that, Ting's lectures were not very informative and felt bland and disengaging, since she would mostly read directly off her slideshow, providing little to no further explanation on the topics. She also was unable to answer questions people had during lecture, making it hard to learn challenging topics. I got straight C's on the first two exams without relying on Justin's notes; I only went to her lecture and looked at her slides. Later in the semester, I decided to go to Justin's lectures, and I've never looked back since. His lectures were much more engaging and he broke down each algorithm in a very simple manner. I finally felt like I understood the content that was being taught. I got a much higher score on the third midterm just from going to Justin's class. This could be observed in the fact that Justin's lecture retention through the semester was pretty high; many seats were filled towards the middle/end of the semester, whereas there were only about 30 people in Ting's lecture out of 200+ students towards the middle of the semester. Overall, I'd say only take her if Justin's seats are filled, and if you don't want to suffer with Kruskal. She's not a great professor, but looking at Justin's notes and attending his class will save you the struggle instead of using her resources.

Professor Ting is a fantastic instructor. She is extremely organized and always willing to help her students succeed. Her slides are very informative and are posted on Canvas, which makes it easy to follow along and review material. The class is structured very well with clear expectations, and there are no surprises when it comes to assignments or exams. If you're ever confused about the homework, I highly recommend going to her office hours—she’s approachable and explains concepts thoroughly. While the class itself can be challenging, Professor Ting’s clear teaching style and support make a big difference. With some effort, you’ll do great. Overall, I highly recommend taking her class!

I don't think Ting's lectures are as bad as people made them out to be but her biggest problem was that she sometimes made difficult concepts more complicated to learn. So the best thing you can do is briefly read Justin's notes before going to her lecture, because she will on occasion, give hints on exams Her exams are very fair, and there are no curveballs (higher averages than Justin's) And she's very helpful in office hours if you need help whether it's lecture material or exams Oh yeah she has snacks in office hours and you're free to take some Overall I think she's fine and it's just her 2nd semester I'm sure she'll improve later on

Professor Kruskal is a good lecturer, a nice man to talk to, and very knowledgeable on the topic of algorithms. However, in terms of class structure, grading, and anything administrative, the class is an absolute disaster. The exam material is not taught in class, rather in prerecorded online lectures (so if you want to go to his in-person classes you effectively double your time spent in class), currently only 19/52 assignments in the course have been graded with under 2 weeks until the final, so it is extremely difficult to get a gauge on how you are doing in the course (which isn't helped by the fact that the averages for the exams have been a 52 and a 63, so there is also a curve you need to take into account), and Prof. Kruskal himself seems unwilling to ever change these things about the course, for reasons he will share with his students if they are so inclined. Overall, stay ahead of the curve, use past homework to study for the exams, and you will do okay in the course, but it is not a fun experience. I truly think Kruskal could have a much higher reputation if he just began covering exam content in person, but it doesn't seem like that will change any time soon.

She's fine...? She has an accent, but you'll get used to it. Ting and Justin are co-teaching (at least for this year) so you share a lot of the homeworks/exams. I just can't say I learned much from her, sorry :( - 3 midterms. - 1 final. - Around 11 homeworks. Only counts the top 10 homeworks. - Around 14 mini multiple-choice quizzes due every Friday except on exam days. Drops the lowest 3? 2?? Idk I can't count.

He's a fine, default professor I'd say. His teaching is really straightforward and simple. He doesn't go too easy or too hard and his exams are really fair (EXCEPT THE FINAL) and the quizzes are trivial when you have a TA that basically gives you the answers beforehand. He curved everyone's grades insanely hard after everyone bombed the final he told us not to worry about so I really appreciate him for that because I KNOW I didn't earn this A, lol. People say he's boring but I don't think so. He teaches what's in the book and his exams don't throw curveballs. If he says "you'll have to do something like this on the exam" then chances are that exact question is word-for-word on the exam. All of this is an exception from the final literally do not trust what he says about the final because he straight up LIED to us about it. And then curved my B- into an A somehow, so.. I don't mind him too much. Soft recommend.

I was a little bit harsh in my last review. but you have to go to lecture, simply looking over her posted notes is not enough because they are often missing important things she covered during lecture/on exams. She's nice though, just not organized on Canvas.

I had him for fall 2025 Lectures are pretty boring, he mostly just reads from the textbook and stumbles over his words frequently, so its a good idea to have the textbook open while attending. He didn't collect homework this semester, but had frequent quizzes which were pretty much rehashed homework questions. As long as you do the homework, you'll be fine for the quizzes. Midterm exams were fair, but he threw a curveball for the final making it much harder than it probably should have. People below mentioned what he did so I'm not gonna repeat it here. At least he gave us a nice curve to compensate for it. tldr: If you have no other options, he's fine, but I wouldn't be jumping at the opportunity to take him again.

Hatice is very nice, I like her. But it's always very difficult to stay engaged during lecture, in part due to her style. This definitely isn't the hardest course in the world, and has a decent enough curve, but the exams were definitely some of the harder things I've taken.

In the second half of the semester, he got so much better. His midterm 2 was really good and he gave us a practice exam that was very similar to the actual exam. He also told us exactly what kind of problems would be on the exam. His final exam was extremely easy, it was almost a copy and paste of the practice exam he gave us before the final exam. It was his first time teaching so the first half of the semester was rough, but he listened to the students advice and really turned things around. The bad reviews were mostly all from the first half of the semester after the first midterm which went horribly.

A bit of context, this is now a post-semester review being written roughly a week after final grades locked in. I already left a mid-semester review, where I gave Zheng only 4 stars to balance out the immediate 1-star review that someone left only 3 weeks into the semester. Now giving my afterthoughts: As the more recent reviews have already said, Zheng definitely improved over the semester, so I won’t restate everything they covered. I mainly want to give some context for why the early reviews were so harsh. After Midterm 1 (with an average of ~76), the GroupMe turned into an echo chamber where a vocal group of students circulated the same complaints for weeks. One of those students made a Google doc listing the issues with Zheng, and people in the chat were explicitly encouraging others to leave 1-star PlanetTerp reviews based on these complaints. That doc later got cleaned up into a printed letter addressed directly to Zheng, asking whether he could provide extra credit, more practice problems, drop the lowest midterms, etc. This letter was then left at the back of class for students to sign if they wanted to. To be fair, I’m not sure Zheng would have become as lenient as he did if that letter hadn’t happened, so some of that pushback was probably warranted, even if it shouldn’t take something like that for changes to happen in a class. Personally, I thought the Midterm 1 practice was definitely enough. I skipped a few problems that clearly weren’t exam-relevant, but most of them lined up with lecture examples or homework. Some questions were basically the same concept with different numbers. I get why people were frustrated, but a lot of the “nothing like the practice” claims were overstated. For Midterm 2 and the final, the practice was even closer to the exams; arguably too close in some cases. Zheng's sections were originally meant to be taught by Anurag Dey, who is known for being an "easy A" according to his PlanetTerp reviews for STAT401. We got switched to Zheng about a month before the fall semester started (i.e., during summer break), so it's possible that the same people were expecting that kind of class and were frustrated when the difficulty didn't match those expectations. I agree with another recent review that 3.5/5 is probably a more accurate rating, but I’m giving a 4 just to round up and reflect the improvement and to help improve the average. Zheng has shown he has the capacity to improve, communicates fairly well given the language barrier, and is certainly not the "worst" choice for STAT400 as other reviewers had initially put it.

As said by other people, this class started out really rough. It was a pain and honestly I thought about dropping it more than a few times. However, after a letter and some feedback from the class, he did improve quite a bit. Definitely his first time teaching this course and it showed. He took lots of feedback and added curves, bonus points, and made the final pretty fair. In the future he has also promised to hand out bonus points in class for questions and such so make sure to participate. He has a bit of a thick accent so I would look at the textbook/youtube a bit. Pretty hard class, but mainly because it was his find time teaching it.
Hes ok

Hatice seems very nice and sweet. She brought us chocolate once. The grade cutoff allowed me to have an A with 90%. But the final being 40% and all tests being 92% is kinda crazy. Also unfortunately I was always extremely bored and lecture was usually really hard to follow along. I did well by taking notes on the textbook sections based on what she decided to teach in lecture (she posts notes online too). To prepare for tests, just do all of the old exams since they're structured similarly.

I think it was his first time teaching and the course started off rough. The lectures were all over the place and the homework’s and first exam were very hard. After that though he definitely improved. He listened to feedback, curved the exams if it was below an 83 avg. He added bonus points to homework’s and gave us better practice. He is overall a nice guy but has room for improvement.

I'd say i'm the average-slightly below student. I got below the means on all exams. Exam 1 mean was 73, i got a 68, Exam 2 mean was 74, i got a 73, Final exam mean was a 133/200 (66), i got a 118/200 (59). Canvas said i got a 67.99% in the class, but i ended with a B-. He curved the hell out of the grades. Exam 1 and 2 were really fair content wise, if you did the quizzes, did the HW, it wouldve been really easy for you, i just freeze when it comes to exams. However, the final exam, was crazy, didnt really put much of the earlier topics you'd expect on there, and heavily focused on later end material with 2/6 questions being insane . It was 100% more difficult in my opinion then previous final exams. BUT, i would recommend him for this class. His quizzes are word for word based off the hw questions, just different numbers and slightly different wording (sometimes its word for word the same). If you do the homeworks which is just questions from the textbook, you will easily get a 100% on these, i averaged like a 89% on them. He teaches pretty well in the beginning, he sometimes get confusing as time goes on, especially if the topic is slightly more difficult because he just goes off textbook definitions. He does welcome questions, he stops the lecture several times to make sure no ones confused obviously were all too afraid to ask. You can 100% self teach yourself on this course though if you wanted. Watch youtube videos, look at the textbook (YT is better for the more complex ones), do the HW's, quizzes, attend OH if you cant find good enough YT vids for a HW, join the groupME for the class for any specific things he couldve said in the lecture about upcoming quizzes/exams, you'll be fine, trust.

I want to preface by saying she is a very nice lady and actually does want her students to do well. However, I don't recommend taking her. The material is honestly covered very fast in the lecture, she gives a lot of examples with the work, but notes are all over the place, the lecture is difficult to understand. For the calculus related part of the course she explained things well but everything related to statistics and theory was rough. Her handwriting is not great which makes it hard to write down the notes. She will answer questions thoroughly if you ask her after the lecture and that can be helpful. Exams are worth 92% of the grade, it's a 400 level class so this might be a given but the exams (at least for me) required A LOT of studying, more than all my other classes put together. There are some practice exams but answer keys are incomplete and very disorganized. The discussions are very lackluster and not helpful. It's required because you have to turn in your homework in person which is annoying. The TA will spend 15 minutes writing down the previous week's homework answers on the board instead of just posting them on canvas and taking questions in office hours. This would take away a lot of time from asking questions about the current week's material and we would almost never get through the worksheet that is supposed to be the focus of the discussion. To add on, the other professor teaching this semester gave a cheat sheet on exams and allowed calculators, our class had neither of those. There are a bunch of formulas to memorize. The only way to do well in this course unless you are just gifted at statistics is to lock in on the textbook. Do as many textbook problems as you can and you will do good on the exam. Overall, I recommend taking the class with a different professor. The grade cutoffs were very generous though.

The lectures were pretty unhelpful, I went to them all and rarely began to understand what he was saying until reading the textbook and doing practice problems. He also says "um" multiple times in every sentence which makes following along challenging. His exams are much more challenging than the homework's so it is really hard to practice doing problems with exam level difficulty. His syllabus says that you will have a formula sheet, which ended up only being true for the final which was a little shocking when going into the first exam. The final exam was so challenging that he did not even release the scores because he had to give such a diabolical curve due to the difficulty of the exam. This class honestly ended up testing my calculus more than my statistical skills. My main problem with this is that none of the homeworks involved calculus that was as advanced as his exams, so i didn't know I needed to brush up on things relating to calc until i was actually taking the final.

Genuinely my goat. Listened to students feedback midway and started to give extra credits in difficult HW & gave a really reasonable, easy final. For midterm 1 and 2, it WAS NOT bad as he put really similar content in exam practice problems and reviewed the topics that will be covered, and the same for final too. I believe he will fix lecture part in following semesters considering it's his first semester teaching and he listened to students feedback.

This guy is overhated, I would give him a 3.5/5 but I'm rounding up because I feel his current rating is unfair. The semester did get off to a rocky start but he quickly improved his teaching and I felt the lectures were easy enough to understand. The homeworks are mostly easy with only one hard proof question per homework with lecture examples being able to guide you. The homeworks usually did enough to prepare for the exams. The quizzes were super easy and were free grade boosters. He's also a super nice guy, he was willing to curve every exam if the average was below 83% and gave so much extra credit on homeworks that my average homework grade ended up being 115%. Also dude was super dripped out every class so take him if you need fashion advice.

Always got the tuffest fits on and his tests are free lol. Curve is very generous, didn't go to lecture and just studied with chat. Also literally gave us the type of problems to expect on midterm 2 and the final (and you made your own cheatsheet for the final). Probably easiest STAT400 class ever, and you don't have to go to lecture

He's not a very good lecturer, and his notes are confusing to read. The quizzes are pretty simple and the exams are okay since he posts the practice problems. But reviewing the practice problems is honestly the only way to learn the material, I wouldn't say he really taught anything effectively. He seems understanding though.

I think I'm of the minority opinion but I thought his lecture style was ok. You definitely need to put some time outside of class to understand the content though. Quizzes are super easy and I think the midterms were fair, but other people definitely have different opinions. The final is 40% of our final grade and it was absolute hell, but he gave us a gigantic curve so we all wound up getting a higher grade than expected. He's a prof will actually curve, which I can't say about like any of my math or cs professors I've had so far. Very nice dude who cares about your GPA, but don't expect the lectures to be enough to actually understand what's happening.

He's not the worst professor, and a lot of the reviews below are probably a little harsh. Early in the semester it was a little rough, with him leaning into theory on the harder side, but he began using more example problems to clear up concept confusion. He gave homeworks that were definitely tough, but began to give lots of bonus point questions on them. He also curved every exam to an 83 average. I think he can definitely improve on his teaching, but it is definitely possible to do well in the class if you practice concepts.

It was his first time teaching STAT400, and he didn't know how to format exams, curves, and lectures. He's a nice guy, though his lectures are pretty boring, and most people ended up skipping the lectures towards the end of the semester. The quizzes are simple(depending on the TA). I ended up skipping most of the lectures and going over the practice questions that he gave us, and did decently on most of the exams(around a 85% average). He also gives bonus points on homework towards the end of the semester, and some quizzes get dropped. The final exam was just a copy of the practice exam he gave us; some of the questions were exactly like the practice exam questions. From what I saw, most of the class felt good about the final and thought it was the easiest exam they had taken. I'm sure in the future his class will be more organized, and the lectures will be more thorough. Overall, I would take him again because(at least for me) it was an easy A.

At first, the course was unreasonably difficult and very disorganized. I think this professor is just new to teaching, so it was a rocky start. Near the end of the course, he improved significantly in his lectures and on top of that, the last few exams were more representitive of the material covered. The homework used to be insanely difficult too, but near the end it was starting to become more managable. I think there is still room for improvement, but throughout the semester he actively worked to improve the course based on feedback.

I'm writing this review just after the final exam for Stat 400. It being his first time teaching at the university, Zheng started the year off rough, with confusing lectures, he was initially bad at explaining topics and gave no insight into what was going to be on each exam. After students expressed their complaints, he really cleaned up his act. For exam #2, he told us what type of problems would be on the exam and gave out practice problems similar to ones on the exam. He also began to give out extra credit points on the homework, which made it easier to recover points. For the final, we were allowed a double sided a4 sheet of notes, and he gave us in-depth practice problems that were very relevant to the problems on the final. I'm giving him a 4 star review because he really turned his teaching style around and doesn't deserve many of the 1 star reviews he was given by students early in the semester, when he was still acclimating to being a teacher for the first time. He also responds quickly to emails and is generally a pretty funny guy in lecture. Please don't shy away from taking his class because of the people who gave him low ratings early in the semester who were upset by their exam 1 results.

This guy really likes math. I mean really likes math. He goes on lots of tangents and tends to conceptualize things in a way that only makes sense if you read the textbook or if you're very tapped into math. Besides the fact that his teaching ability is below average, he does actually care about his students. He curves when necessary and gives you homeworks that are free points along with bonus points that you can go for to save your grade. If you actually study, you can succeed in this class but it is hard to learn from this guy. The only reason he gets 3 stars is because his fits are pretty fire.
I thought the final would be fair and simple given that I did pretty well the whole year (94%), the past exams weren't too bad, and the reviews say his exams are easy... I was wrong.

terrible, just terrible, final hit harder than a truck. his final comparatively was harder than any past finals given, he made us think it was going to be way easier. I had 98 on first mid term and a 80 on second one and I'm still probably going to fail. just think abt that.

I watched my degree evaporate before my eyes on the final. Please don't take this man if your life depends on it. His exams were nothing like the HW, quizzes, or practice exams he gave us. He told us point estimators were not going to be a major topic of the final since we only learned it for a week, but then proceeded to make it 25% of the final exam, which is worth 40% of your grade. Pincus is a nice dude though and will answer all your questions. He also only reads off the slides and his lectures aren't more helpful than looking through a textbook

This will be an overall holistic review of his teaching through his first semester and some lore n stuff, this is gonna be fun to write. Week 1 - 3: (Intro stuff, basic set theory, combinations, counting (discrete math stuff), geometric probability, sigma algebra (wtf is that), conditional probability, and some Bayes theorem) Kinda bad, didn't have good examples through class, but wasn't the worst professor ever, started to record lectures with Notability, used the microphone (so ppl in the back of the room can hear him), and has PDFs of the notes after 2-3 weeks. Pretty rough start, but we got there. Week 4 - 6 (First Exam on Week 6) (More Bayes, Random Variables, pmf + cdf for discrete RV's, variance, binomial, geometric, and hypergeometric) Up to this point was decent, I learnt way more through the textbook and Khan Academy and videos, so yeah, also this is when I stopped going to class for a bit (all the career fair stuff was happening n shit) (The practice problems for exam 1 was basically just a random document with 50 easy questions shoved in right the Monday before the exam, just went through what we learnt up to the point) Week 7 - 10 (Fall break + negative binomial, poisson, continuous RVs, pdf, cdf, expected values, and variance. Normal + standard normal distributions, transformation of RV, exponential + gamma + weibull + log normal + gamma distribution) This week we had to go to the hotel cuz idk wtf the be doing in the Armory. This is when he turned around and became the GOAT, went to his office hours a lot between exam 1 and 2 to ask for him to help us a bit with either doing practice problems, bonus points, practice, or (Telling us exactly what type of questions would be). He said he'll think about it. We also signed a petition to ask for like either it was like bonus points or smt or like a review. And he was like (damn these kids lmao, but he did help us tho). The Tuesday right before exam 2, he said, "You have 5 questions. These are the topics to study up on, kiddos." It definitely helped me, which was nice. There was like 1 problem on negative hypergeometric (we didn't learn it, but we had to try and derive it; most ppl got 13/20 on it was super lenient on grading). Also, week 10, he started to do bonus points on the HW yeaaaa!!! Week (11 - 13): (Joint RV, Joint pdf, marginal pdf, conditional pdf, expected values, covariance and correlation, some intro statistic stuff, point estimation, methods of point estimation (Method of Moments) He started to do curves this week (thank the heavens). Other than that, this week was pretty chill. The examples were at least starting to get better, and he kinda explained like why they mattered and what it kinda means in the bigger picture. We also had the opportunity for a 2.5% grade bonus in class on the Tuesday before Thanksgiving Break. (Curve method linear transformation with the average score and max score if avg less than 83, curve up) Exam 1 Cuve = new score = 0.708 x old score + 29.17 Exam 2 Curve = new score = 0.7831 x old score + 21.69 Final Exam Curve (Maybe if we get a lower than 83% class average) Week (14 - 15) (Hypothesis testing, linear regression, MLE estimator) Slower week, was nothing crazy, we got the practice for the final 2 weeks before the final Sunday at 12:34 am, I also emailed him at 3:00 pm Sunday and said he would give it after he got home, my ass was waiting for him to post it. TAs kinda did the funny with the quizzes, and the professor had to fix their problems with not creating the same quizzes n shit, but yea We'll see for the final exam and if any new things happen, but he said that he would make the final exam easier and only 8 problems, so it should be good (I hope) Exam 1 Stats 76 avg and the highest grade was 100 Exam 2 Stats 78.29 avg and the highest grade was 100 So, not the worst and definitely got better, I guess if you can self-teach yourself first, then read his lecture recording/notes after you understand the topic, then you can ask some questions and maybe pick out some more things better in the lecture. I think he's pretty chill and yeah :thumbs_up: Hopes this helps anyone for Spring 2025 or any future semesters of STAT400 he teaches, and I hope he continues to enjoy teaching and being nice to his students Don't wanna glaze him or hate on him too much, but I think a 3 or 4 is a good rating for him.

Some things to note about the other reviews for this class, most of them were made after the first midterm. which was quite difficult and had a pretty low average. After some discussing, Professor Zheng agreed to add a curve to all the exams and the final and it was a pretty nice curve too. He also made exam 2 much easier than exam 1 and gave much better practice material, so most of these reviews are complaining about an un-curved, difficult, ill-prepared exam you won't get. About the class itself, the homework is quite difficult, much much harder than the exams or anything else you will see in class except a few examples. Professor Zheng gives out a lot of bonus points on the homework however, which makes getting a 100% in the homework category quite easy. There are weekly quizzes in discussion written by the TA's, but these are very easy compared to everything else. The review guides are pretty comprehensive and covered everything on exam 2. We did not finish all the content in the class (didn't make it to one topic) but covered almost everything so you'll still learn. I also think this class should definitely have MATH240 as a prereq, but the professor nerfed all the joint-pdf questions so knowledge from that class isn't required. Overall, STAT400 is a difficult class but Professor Zheng did a good job (in the second half of the semester) of teaching and preparing you for the exams. Take most of the reviews before the middle of November or with a W with a grain of salt, as these are from before the curve and massive improvement in Professor Zheng's teaching and preparation material.

Responded well to criticism and made changes in the middle of the semester to accommodate for his students. For example, the homework at the beginning of the semester was very hard compared to what he makes now. The homework is much more relevant to what we are doing in class, and he created bonus questions for those of us who want a bump to our grade. Another example is how he helped us prepare for midterm 2. Although he released the practice questions a little late, they were very helpful for what we would see on the test. I went from a <50% midterm 1 grade, to just barely a 90% on midterm 2. Also, he curved exams that had an average below 83 generously. To everyone reading all of his reviews and preparing for the worst, I promise you its not that bad at all. Some people are just coping such as the review below mines.

Not as bad as the other reviews say at all, was a little bit rough at first but he improved as a lecturer throughout the course. Also listened to student feedback, helped us prepare better for midterm 2, eventually curved Midterms 1 and 2, and added bonus points on homework. Nice guy, sometimes a little confusing examples but provided more relevant and easy to understand examples. His first time teaching and he definitely got better throughout, 1 star rating is 100% undeserved. Also does have fire fits every class.
Lectures are just reading off textbook definition. However, he is nice and answers to quizzes are given before taking it.

As stated below, this man should be teaching a fashion class. THET465 would be a better fit for his teaching. He has very nice outfits but is not an effective teacher. Homework is too difficult and the exams don't match the content taught in class.

Pros: dresses nice, review guides are very similar to the exams so if you diligently do them, you shouldnt struggle on the exams, the quizzes are free points so even though he doesnt tell us the content of the quiz, its simple if you do the homework(lowkey on depends ta tho, my TA was chill, I've heard that the other TA was lowkey bad). He posts the notes, with his voice recording over them, so if you miss lectures, it's not the end of the world. The best thing is that he curves graciously. The average for our second exam was 78; however, he wants the exam averages to be 83, so he curves them up to it. If you do poorly on an exam, these curves are lifesavers. He genuinely tries to ensure that we all do well in this class and listens to us. Cons: homework is pretty hard, I won't lie, but he also gives us a week to do them, and he encourages collaboration with your peers, just don't cheat on it. Regarding my grade, I don't study enough, which is entirely on me; nothing on him. Another con is that there may be gaps in your understanding due to inconsistencies in the notes. Since he is using other people's notes for this class, that may be the reason for these gaps, which could improve in future semesters, as this is his first semester teaching it. Final: These complaints stem from the fact that a 400-level math course is not easy. Shocker!!!. Just be prepared to do the work. He is 3.5, not a 4, but since there is not 3.5, I'm rounding up for my guy. Stop the hate!!!!!

Not nearly as bad as the reviews here would suggest. Was admittedly a somewhat poor lecturer the first few weeks with convoluted examples, but definitely improved throughout the course of the semester. Homeworks also only have max 1 proof/theoretical question, the rest are mostly computational and not as hard as suggested. Honestly did not find exams 1 and 2 that hard, even with no prior Stats experience and moderate studying, going to class is key imo even people say otherwise. Zheng also listened to feedback, ended up curving midterms, and provided better material for exam 2. Overall not the best lecturer in the world and still makes things a little confusing at times but got better as the year went on and is an open and nice professor. Take everything else read here with a grain of salt

Hatice is a pretty good professor. She is not very responsive to emails, but if you walk up to her after class to ask her a question, she will give you good information-- for example, I missed one of the weekly quizzes and emailed her to see if I could retake it, and she did not respond, but when i asked her after class, she was very responsive and let me take the quiz late with no penalty. Her lectures can be a little dry, but they are very example heavy and give you a good grasp of studying. That said, she does speed through the actual content. All in all, if you are diligent about reading the textbook and pay attention to the practice problems she goes over in class, you should perform well in the class and come out with a good understanding of statistics.
Sadly she doesn’t answer emails or help at all and her lectures are boring. Pretty bad teacher if you have another choice please choose them

he looks super cool walks into the lecture hall with a fireeee fit and then opens his mouth and everything goes downhill. cannot teach, doesn't give anything to help prepare for exams until like 1-2 days before, and the class is a mess. nobody should be stressing over stat400 like this lol do not take him!!!! he is kind though so at least there's that. but yea i have no clue what im about to get in this class bc he hasn't told us if/how he's curving the class 😹😹😹 just don't take him
Crazy hard homework and exams that have no similarity to anything done in class or quizzes. Really poor lectures and exam weighting that will drop your A+ to a C- in no time.

Horrible teacher, extremely boring and would often skip over questions and what she would go over in class would not be actually posted. She would just erase her messy notes and then post like a super bare bones version. Completely changed up her policy for her last schedule, it's actually ridiculous.

During lecture he doesn't go over anything useful. You will leave lecture knowing nothing. On top of that, the homework contains the most difficult quetstions related to the given topic. So if you are only relying on the homeworks you will never come away with even a hint of a foundational understanding of the homework topics. Everything I just went over are all of the resources you will be provided with in this course. If this is the only professor available, just don't do it for your own sake. He has some serious drip though, I can't lie.

Usually I do not leave reviews until the course is over, but so far this class is a total MESS. The homework is insanely hard competition math - style questions that are graded on accuracy with no preparation for them. We have weekly quizzes that are pretty easy but there is basically no way to prepare for them and they vary based on TA so this class has one TA that gives easy quizzes while another that gives harder quizzes. Finally, his exam preparation is literally non existent. Does not teach great but has very nice outfits. Would recommend to teach fashion class instead of stat.

Does not go over any practice problems. Just yaps about a bunch of formulas and theories. He did a review session before the midterm and said doing practice problems would not be a good use of our time and instead just yapped about some theoretical stuff that he already yapped about before. This man is a nightmare and should not be teaching this class. Run for you life.

goes over no practice problems in class, therefore, leaving us clueless for homeworks and exams. we are 3 weeks behind but he refuses to push the second midterm back. take anyone else but him. this class should not be this much of a struggle.

He is the worst option possible for stat400. Take any other professor available over him. I would even suggest delaying this class a semester so you do not have to go through the pain and torture he puts you through.

Professor Richard, who looks slightly vicious, is a lovely person who is highly knowledgeable in optimization, linear algebra, calculus, and probability theory. You may sometimes see him telling jokes with no expression during the lecture, and he is very patient with every student when you ask questions during office hours, class breaks, and class. However, I admit that some course concepts are hard to comprehend at first glance. Still, after reviewing the in-class material and combining it with the external video associated with that particular concept, the difficulty of the homework should be acceptable. As for the midterm and final, he will provide some review material and the review section. If you follow the pattern of the review materials, combined with some additional practice in linear algebra, both exams are good to go. Furthermore, for the final project, I recommend consolidating the topic and working on the solution as soon as possible; it may take longer than you expected. Professor Richard has the best mathematic expertise I have ever encountered at the University of Maryland. Eventually, he will automatically and dynamically adjust the grade scope according to the entire class's performance, so do not worry too much. As the final reminder, he will review the concept of linear algebra in optimization during the first several lectures; if you are not familiar with the relative concept, please try to catch it, which will make the rest of the lecture manageable.

Don't expect to learn anything, the course content is Python101. Don't trust a word he says about what will be on the exam, every one of them has at least one "gotchya" question that, at times, requires explicit quoting from the slides (not kidding). Your best bet is to read the slides if you want, but be absolutely sure to print every single one of them out for the open-book exams, because any other notes you bring will be worthless. The only A- I received in the program, not because I lacked the conceptual knowledge, but because I didn't have the slides on-hand to quote from, and I trusted him when he said we didn't need to regurgitate them. Don't get got. Print the slides.
